2021.8.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2021) — Model Solution


Part (a): AR($p$), MA($q$), ARMA($p$,$q$) and ARIMA($p$,$d$,$q$) Processes

Reference: Box, Jenkins & Reinsel, Time Series Analysis, 4th ed., Ch. 3–6; Brockwell & Davis, Time Series: Theory and Methods, 2nd ed., Ch. 3–4.

1. Process Definitions

AR($p$): $\phi(B)y_t=\varepsilon_t$: $y_t=\phi_1y_{t-1}+\cdots+\phi_py_{t-p}+\varepsilon_t$. Stationary iff roots of $\phi(z)=1-\phi_1z-\cdots-\phi_pz^p=0$ are outside the unit circle. ACF decays exponentially/oscillates; PACF cuts off after lag $p$.

MA($q$): $y_t=\theta(B)\varepsilon_t$: $y_t=\varepsilon_t+\theta_1\varepsilon_{t-1}+\cdots+\theta_q\varepsilon_{t-q}$. Always stationary; invertible iff roots of $\theta(z)$ are outside the unit circle. ACF cuts off after lag $q$; PACF decays.

ARMA($p$,$q$): $\phi(B)y_t=\theta(B)\varepsilon_t$. Combines AR and MA. ACF and PACF both tail off.

ARIMA($p$,$d$,$q$): $\phi(B)(1-B)^d y_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$. The series $y_t$ is differenced $d$ times to achieve stationarity before fitting ARMA($p$,$q$).

2. Box-Jenkins Methodology: Finding $p$, $d$, $q$

  1. Identification of $d$: Test for unit roots (ADF, PP, KPSS tests). If non-stationary, difference until stationary. Count differences as $d$.
  2. Identification of $p$ and $q$: Examine the ACF and PACF of the differenced series:
    • ACF cuts off at lag $q$, PACF tails off → MA($q$)
    • PACF cuts off at lag $p$, ACF tails off → AR($p$)
    • Both tail off → ARMA($p$,$q$); try low-order models
  3. Model selection criteria: AIC $= -2\ln L + 2(p+q+1)$; BIC $= -2\ln L + (p+q+1)\ln n$. Choose model minimising AIC/BIC.

3. Parameter Estimation of ARIMA

  • Method of Moments (Yule-Walker): For AR($p$): solve $\hat{\phi}=\hat{\Gamma}_p^{-1}\hat{\gamma}_p$ where $\hat{\Gamma}_p$ is the sample autocovariance matrix. Simple but not fully efficient.
  • Conditional Least Squares: Minimise $\sum_{t=p+1}^T \varepsilon_t^2$ treating initial observations as fixed. Computationally simple.
  • Maximum Likelihood (ML): Assuming $\varepsilon_t\overset{\text{iid}}{\sim}N(0,\sigma^2)$, maximise the likelihood via Kalman filter recursion (exact Gaussian likelihood). Most efficient; standard in practice.

4. Diagnostic Checking

Examine residuals: (i) Ljung-Box test for remaining autocorrelation: $Q=n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^m\hat{\rho}_k^2/(n-k)\sim\chi^2(m-p-q)$. (ii) Normal Q-Q plot. (iii) ACF/PACF of residuals should show no significant lags.

[Q.E.D.]
2021.8.2:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2021) — Model Solution


Part (b): Dickey-Fuller (DF) Test for Unit Root

Reference: Dickey & Fuller (1979, 1981), JASA; Enders, W. (2015), Applied Econometric Time Series, 4th ed., Ch. 4; Hamilton, J.D. (1994), Time Series Analysis, Ch. 17; Gujarati & Porter, Basic Econometrics, 5th ed., Ch. 21.

1. Definition: Dickey-Fuller Test

The DF test examines whether an autoregressive process has a unit root. Consider the AR(1) model:

$$y_t = \rho y_{t-1} + u_t, \quad u_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim}(0,\sigma^2)$$

$H_0$: $\rho=1$ (unit root; series is I(1)) vs $H_1$: $|\rho|<1$ (stationary series). Rewriting by subtracting $y_{t-1}$:

$$\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + u_t, \quad \delta = \rho-1$$

$H_0$: $\delta=0$ vs $H_1$: $\delta<0$. The DF test statistic is:

$$\tau = \frac{\hat{\delta}}{SE(\hat{\delta})}$$

2. Three Variants of the DF Test

ModelEquationAppropriate when
1$\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1}+u_t$Zero mean process
2$\Delta y_t = c+\delta y_{t-1}+u_t$Non-zero mean (with drift)
3$\Delta y_t = c+bt+\delta y_{t-1}+u_t$Trend-stationary alternative

3. How to Apply the DF Test

  1. Plot the series to determine whether to include drift or trend.
  2. Run the OLS regression for the appropriate model; obtain $\hat{\delta}$ and its $SE$.
  3. Compute $\tau=\hat{\delta}/SE(\hat{\delta})$.
  4. Compare $\tau$ with the DF critical values (MacKinnon 1991) — these are more negative than normal $t$-critical values under $H_0$. E.g., at 5%, $\tau_{cr}\approx-2.86$ (with constant), $-3.41$ (with constant and trend).
  5. Reject $H_0$ (unit root) if $\tau < \tau_{cr}$ (i.e., $|\tau|$ is large enough in the negative direction).

4. Augmented DF (ADF) Test

Add lagged differences to control serial correlation in $u_t$:

$$\Delta y_t = c + \delta y_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^p\gamma_j\Delta y_{t-j} + u_t$$

Choose $p$ by AIC/BIC or sequential testing (Ng-Perron, 2001).

5. Characterising a Unit Root Process

If $H_0$ is not rejected ($\delta=0$), then $y_t$ is I(1) — integrated of order 1:

  • Random Walk (no drift): $y_t=y_{t-1}+u_t$. Variance grows linearly: $\text{Var}(y_t)=t\sigma^2\to\infty$. ACF $\to 1$ for all lags.
  • Random Walk with drift: $y_t=c+y_{t-1}+u_t$. Long-run upward/downward drift of $ct$.
  • Spurious regression risk: Regressing two independent I(1) series yields spurious $R^2$ and $t$-statistics.
  • Remedy: First-difference $\Delta y_t=y_t-y_{t-1}$ to obtain a stationary I(0) series before inference. Alternatively, check for cointegration (Engle-Granger, Johansen).
[Q.E.D.]
2021.8.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2021) — Model Solution


Part (c): Autocovariance Matrix and Partial Autocorrelation Bound for $n=3$

Reference: Brockwell & Davis, Time Series: Theory and Methods, 2nd ed., §1.6, §3.3; Box, Jenkins & Reinsel, Ch. 2; Anderson, T.W. (1971), The Statistical Analysis of Time Series, Ch. 8.

1. Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Functions

For a stationary process $\{y_t\}$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t+h}) = E[(y_t-\mu)(y_{t+h}-\mu)]$$ $$\rho(h) = \frac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)}, \quad |\rho(h)|\leq 1$$

2. Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Matrices of Order $n$

The autocovariance matrix of order $n$ is the $n\times n$ Toeplitz matrix:

$$\Gamma_n = \begin{pmatrix}\gamma(0)&\gamma(1)&\cdots&\gamma(n-1)\\\gamma(1)&\gamma(0)&\cdots&\gamma(n-2)\\ \vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\\gamma(n-1)&\gamma(n-2)&\cdots&\gamma(0)\end{pmatrix}$$

The autocorrelation matrix $R_n = \Gamma_n/\gamma(0)$ has $(i,j)$ entry $\rho(|i-j|)$. For a valid stationary process, $\Gamma_n$ (and hence $R_n$) must be positive semi-definite.

3. Case $n=3$

$$R_3 = \begin{pmatrix}1&\rho_1&\rho_2\\\rho_1&1&\rho_1\\\rho_2&\rho_1&1\end{pmatrix}$$

4. Proof: $-1\leq (\rho_2-\rho_1^2)/(1-\rho_1^2)\leq 1$

Step 1. For $R_3$ to be PSD, all principal minors must be $\geq 0$.

Leading minors:

  • $|R_1|=1>0$ ✓
  • $|R_2|=1-\rho_1^2>0$ → $|\rho_1|<1$ (from the $2\times 2$ case)
  • $|R_3|=\det(R_3)\geq 0$

Step 2. Compute $\det(R_3)$:

$$\det(R_3) = (1-\rho_1^2)^2 - (\rho_2-\rho_1^2)^2$$

Proof of this factorisation: Expanding directly,

$$\det(R_3) = 1(1-\rho_1^2)-\rho_1(\rho_1-\rho_1\rho_2)+\rho_2(\rho_1^2-\rho_2) = 1-2\rho_1^2+2\rho_1^2\rho_2-\rho_2^2$$

Note: $(1-\rho_1^2)^2-(\rho_2-\rho_1^2)^2=(1-\rho_1^2)^2-\rho_2^2+2\rho_1^2\rho_2-\rho_1^4=1-2\rho_1^2+\rho_1^4-\rho_2^2+2\rho_1^2\rho_2-\rho_1^4=1-2\rho_1^2+2\rho_1^2\rho_2-\rho_2^2$. ✓

Step 3. From $\det(R_3)\geq 0$:

$$(1-\rho_1^2)^2 \geq (\rho_2-\rho_1^2)^2$$ $$|\rho_2-\rho_1^2|\leq |1-\rho_1^2| = 1-\rho_1^2 \quad (\text{since }|\rho_1|<1\Rightarrow 1-\rho_1^2>0)$$ $$-1 \leq \frac{\rho_2-\rho_1^2}{1-\rho_1^2} \leq 1$$

5. Interpretation

The quantity $\phi_{22} = (\rho_2-\rho_1^2)/(1-\rho_1^2)$ is precisely the partial autocorrelation at lag 2 (from the Yule-Walker equations for AR(2)). The result $|\phi_{22}|\leq 1$ is the necessary condition for the AR(2) spectral density to be non-negative, and equivalently for the $3\times 3$ autocorrelation matrix to be PSD.

[Q.E.D.]

2022.2.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (c): EWMA Smoothing, Adaptive Forecasting, and Holt-Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing

Reference: Box, Jenkins, Reinsel & Ljung, Time Series Analysis, 5th ed., Ch. 4; Chatfield, C., The Analysis of Time Series, 6th ed., Ch. 4; Holt, C.C. (1957); Winters, P.R. (1960), Management Science.

1. Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) — Definition

For a time series $\{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_T\}$, the EWMA at time $t$ (also called single exponential smoothing) is defined by the recurrence:

$$S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1 \tag{1}$$

with initialisation $S_1 = X_1$ (or the historical mean). The smoothing constant $\alpha$ controls the weighting: large $\alpha$ gives more weight to recent observations (responsive, less smooth); small $\alpha$ gives more weight to history (smooth, slow to react).

Expanding (1) recursively:

$$S_t = \alpha\sum_{j=0}^{t-1}(1-\alpha)^j X_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^t S_0$$

The weights $\alpha(1-\alpha)^j$ decrease geometrically with lag $j$ — hence exponentially weighted. All weights sum to 1 (for large $t$).

Adaptive Forecasting with EWMA

The one-step-ahead forecast made at time $t$ for time $t+1$ is simply:

$$\hat{X}_{t+1|t} = S_t$$

The forecast error is $e_t = X_t - \hat{X}_{t|t-1} = X_t - S_{t-1}$, so the updating equation becomes:

$$S_t = S_{t-1} + \alpha e_t$$

This is the error-correction form: the new estimate updates the previous forecast by a fraction $\alpha$ of the latest forecast error — the basis of adaptive forecasting. EWMA is optimal (in the MMSE sense) for an IMA(1,1) = ARIMA(0,1,1) process with MA parameter $\theta = 1-\alpha$.

2. When to Use Holt-Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing

Single EWMA (equation 1) is appropriate only for series with no trend and no seasonality. As the level of complexity increases:

  • Holt's Double Exponential Smoothing: Used when the series has a linear trend but no seasonality.
  • Holt-Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing: Used when the series has both a linear trend AND seasonal variation (periodic fluctuations with period $L$, typically $L=12$ for monthly, $L=4$ for quarterly data).

Triple smoothing is the method of choice for retail sales, energy demand, tourist arrivals, and other economic series with regular seasonality.

3. Holt-Winters Method for Multiplicative Seasonality

In the multiplicative model, seasonal fluctuations are proportional to the level — appropriate when the amplitude of seasonal swings grows with the level of the series (e.g., tourism revenue).

The model assumes: $X_t = (L_t + T_t)\,S_t + \varepsilon_t$ approximately, where $L_t$ = level, $T_t$ = trend increment, $S_t$ = seasonal factor.

The three updating equations are:

(i) Level update:

$$L_t = \alpha\frac{X_t}{S_{t-L}} + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}), \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

The current observation $X_t$ is de-seasonalised by dividing by the seasonal index $S_{t-L}$ (from the same season one period ago) before updating the level.

(ii) Trend (slope) update:

$$T_t = \gamma(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma)T_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \gamma < 1$$

The trend is updated as a smoothed estimate of the current change in level.

(iii) Seasonal factor update:

$$S_t = \delta\frac{X_t}{L_t} + (1-\delta)S_{t-L}, \quad 0 < \delta < 1$$

The seasonal index is updated by comparing the current observation to the estimated level.

$h$-step-ahead forecast at time $T$:

$$\hat{X}_{T+h|T} = (L_T + h\,T_T)\,S_{T+h-L}$$

where $h = 1, 2, \ldots$ and $S_{T+h-L}$ is the most recent seasonal index for the same season as $T+h$.

Initialisation: $L_0$, $T_0$, and $S_{1-L}, \ldots, S_0$ are typically initialised using a classical decomposition of the first few years of data (compute trend by regression, seasonal factors by ratio-to-trend).

Parameter estimation: $\alpha, \gamma, \delta$ are chosen to minimise the sum of squared one-step forecast errors $\sum_{t} e_t^2$, typically via Nelder-Mead or grid search optimisation.

Note: The additive Holt-Winters model uses $L_t + S_t$ (and subtracts/adds seasonal effects rather than multiplying/dividing). It is preferred when seasonal amplitude is constant regardless of the level.

[Q.E.D.]
2022.4.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (a): OLS and GLS Estimators — Efficiency with AR(1) Disturbances

Reference: Johnston & DiNardo, Econometric Methods, 4th ed., Ch. 6; Greene, W. H., Econometric Analysis, 8th ed., Ch. 20; Gujarati & Porter, Basic Econometrics, 5th ed., Ch. 12.

1. Model Setup

In deviation-from-mean form:

$$Y_t = \beta X_t + u_t, \quad t = 1,\ldots,n \tag{1}$$ $$u_t = \rho u_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad |\rho|<1, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{IID}(0,\sigma^2) \tag{2}$$

Under AR(1), the covariance structure of $\mathbf{u}$ is $\text{Cov}(u_t, u_s) = \frac{\sigma^2}{1-\rho^2}\rho^{|t-s|}$, i.e., $E(\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}') = \sigma^2 \Omega$ where $\Omega_{ts} = \frac{\rho^{|t-s|}}{1-\rho^2}$.

Part (i): OLS and GLS Estimators of $\beta$

OLS Estimator ($b$):

Minimise $\sum_{t=1}^n (Y_t - \beta X_t)^2$. The OLS estimator is:

$$b = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^n X_t Y_t}{\sum_{t=1}^n X_t^2} = \frac{\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}}{\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}}$$

It is unbiased ($E(b) = \beta$) but not BLUE (Gauss-Markov fails because $E(\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}') \neq \sigma^2 I$). Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(b) = \frac{\sigma^2}{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^2}\mathbf{X}'\Omega\mathbf{X} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}^2}\sum_{t=1}^n\sum_{s=1}^n X_t X_s \frac{\rho^{|t-s|}}{1-\rho^2}$$

where $S_{xx} = \sum X_t^2$.

GLS Estimator ($\hat{\beta}$):

The GLS (Aitken) estimator minimises $(\mathbf{Y}-\beta\mathbf{X})'\Omega^{-1}(\mathbf{Y}-\beta\mathbf{X})$. For AR(1) errors, $\Omega^{-1}$ corresponds to the Prais-Winsten transformation: define $Y_t^* = Y_t - \rho Y_{t-1}$, $X_t^* = X_t - \rho X_{t-1}$ for $t = 2,\ldots,n$ (with an adjusted first observation). Then:

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^n X_t^* Y_t^*}{\sum_{t=2}^n X_t^{*2}} = \frac{\mathbf{X}^{*\prime}\mathbf{Y}^*}{\mathbf{X}^{*\prime}\mathbf{X}^*}$$

By the Gauss-Markov theorem extended to GLS, $\hat{\beta}$ is BLUE. Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{t=2}^n X_t^{*2}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\mathbf{X}^{*\prime}\mathbf{X}^*}$$

since the transformed errors $\varepsilon_t = Y_t^* - \beta X_t^*$ are iid$(0,\sigma^2)$.

Part (ii): Efficiency Ratio $\text{Var}(b)/\text{Var}(\hat{\beta})$

Step 1: Approximate $\text{Var}(b)$.

Expand $\mathbf{X}'\Omega\mathbf{X} = \frac{1}{1-\rho^2}\sum_t\sum_s X_t X_s \rho^{|t-s|}$. Collecting lag terms:

$$\sum_t\sum_s X_t X_s\rho^{|t-s|} = S_{xx} + 2\rho\sum_{tLet $r_1 = \frac{\sum_t X_t X_{t+1}}{S_{xx}}$ be the sample lag-1 autocorrelation of $\{X_t\}$. Ignoring terms of order $O(\rho^k r_1)$ for all $(k+1) > 3$ (i.e., $k \geq 2$), we retain only the first-order correction:

$$\sum_t\sum_s X_t X_s\rho^{|t-s|} \approx S_{xx}(1 + 2\rho r_1)$$ $$\therefore\quad \text{Var}(b) \approx \frac{\sigma^2(1+2\rho r_1)}{(1-\rho^2)\,S_{xx}} \tag{3}$$

Step 2: Approximate $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta})$.

$$\sum_{t=2}^n X_t^{*2} = \sum_t(X_t - \rho X_{t-1})^2 \approx S_{xx} + \rho^2 S_{xx} - 2\rho\,r_1\,S_{xx} = S_{xx}(1 + \rho^2 - 2\rho r_1)$$ $$\therefore\quad \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}(1+\rho^2 - 2\rho r_1)} \tag{4}$$

Step 3: Compute the ratio.

$$\boxed{\frac{\text{Var}(b)}{\text{Var}(\hat{\beta})} = \frac{(1+2\rho r_1)(1+\rho^2-2\rho r_1)}{1-\rho^2}}$$

Expanding the numerator (ignoring $\rho^2 r_1$ and $\rho^3 r_1$ terms as per the given approximation):

$$(1+2\rho r_1)(1+\rho^2-2\rho r_1) \approx 1 + \rho^2 - 2\rho r_1 + 2\rho r_1 = 1+\rho^2$$ $$\frac{\text{Var}(b)}{\text{Var}(\hat{\beta})} \approx \frac{1+\rho^2}{1-\rho^2} \tag{5}$$

Comment on Relative Efficiency

  • For any $|\rho| > 0$: $\frac{1+\rho^2}{1-\rho^2} > 1$, so $\text{Var}(b) > \text{Var}(\hat{\beta})$. GLS is always more efficient than OLS whenever autocorrelation is present ($\rho \neq 0$).
  • As $|\rho| \to 1$: the ratio $\to \infty$, i.e., OLS becomes arbitrarily inefficient relative to GLS.
  • When $\rho = 0$: $\text{Var}(b)/\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = 1$ — OLS and GLS coincide (as expected).
  • The efficiency gain is particularly large when the regressors $X_t$ are highly autocorrelated (large $|r_1|$) and the disturbances are also highly autocorrelated (large $|\rho|$).
[Q.E.D.]

UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (a): MA($q$) Process — ACF, Invertibility, and Order Identification

Reference: Box, Jenkins, Reinsel & Ljung, Time Series Analysis, 5th ed., Chs. 3–4; Brockwell & Davis, Introduction to Time Series and Forecasting, 3rd ed., Ch. 2.

Part (i): MA($q$) Process — Definition and ACF

A moving average process of order $q$, denoted MA($q$), is defined as:

$$X_t = \mu + e_t + \theta_1 e_{t-1} + \theta_2 e_{t-2} + \cdots + \theta_q e_{t-q} \tag{1}$$

where $\{e_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$ (white noise), and $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_q$ are MA parameters. In the backshift operator $B$: $X_t - \mu = \Theta(B)e_t$ where $\Theta(z) = 1 + \theta_1 z + \cdots + \theta_q z^q$.

Properties: An MA($q$) process is always weakly stationary (all moments are finite and time-invariant) regardless of the values of $\theta_j$, since it is a finite linear combination of white noise terms.

Autocovariance Function: For $k = 0, 1, \ldots, q$:

$$\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-k}) = \sigma^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q-k} \theta_j \theta_{j+k}, \quad \theta_0 = 1$$

where the sum uses $E(e_s e_t) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{1}_{s=t}$, so only coincident $e$-terms survive. For $k > q$: $\gamma(k) = 0$.

Autocorrelation Function (ACF):

$$\rho(k) = \frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{\sum_{j=0}^{q-k}\theta_j\theta_{j+k}}{\sum_{j=0}^{q}\theta_j^2}, \quad k = 1, \ldots, q$$ $$\rho(k) = 0 \quad \text{for } k > q$$

Key property: The ACF of an MA($q$) process cuts off (equals zero exactly) after lag $q$. This is the fundamental diagnostic for identifying an MA process.

Part (ii): Writing MA(1) as an Infinite AR Process (Invertibility)

The MA(1) process is $X_t = e_t + \theta e_{t-1}$ (WLOG $\mu = 0$).

Invertibility condition: An MA($q$) process is invertible if and only if all roots of the characteristic polynomial $\Theta(z) = 1 + \theta_1 z + \cdots + \theta_q z^q = 0$ lie strictly outside the unit circle. For MA(1): $\Theta(z) = 1 + \theta z = 0 \Rightarrow z = -1/\theta$. Invertibility requires $|z| > 1$, i.e., $|{-1/\theta}| > 1$, i.e., $|\theta| < 1$.

AR($\infty$) representation: Under $|\theta| < 1$, we can write:

$$e_t = \Theta(B)^{-1}(X_t) = \frac{X_t}{1 + \theta B} = \sum_{j=0}^\infty (-\theta)^j X_{t-j}$$

Since $e_t = X_t + \theta e_{t-1}$, back-substituting recursively:

$$e_t = X_t - \theta X_{t-1} + \theta^2 X_{t-2} - \theta^3 X_{t-3} + \cdots$$ $$\implies X_t = e_t + \theta X_{t-1} - \theta^2 X_{t-2} + \theta^3 X_{t-3} - \cdots$$ $$\boxed{X_t = e_t - \sum_{j=1}^\infty (-\theta)^j X_{t-j} = e_t + \sum_{j=1}^\infty (-1)^{j+1}\theta^j X_{t-j}}$$

This is an AR($\infty$) process with AR coefficients $\pi_j = (-1)^{j+1}\theta^j$. The weights decay geometrically as $|\theta|^j \to 0$, ensuring the series converges. This representation allows expressing current $X_t$ as a function of its entire past, making it possible to estimate $\theta$ from observed data.

Part (iii): Identification and Selection of AR and MA Orders

Model identification uses the sample ACF and PACF together:

ModelACFPACF
AR($p$)Tails off geometrically/sinusoidallyCuts off after lag $p$
MA($q$)Cuts off after lag $q$Tails off geometrically/sinusoidally
ARMA($p$,$q$)Tails off after max($p$,$q$)$-$min($p$,$q$) lagsTails off

The 95% significance bounds for the sample ACF are $\pm 2/\sqrt{T}$ (Bartlett's formula). Significant sample ACF values outside these bounds are potential MA terms; significant PACF values indicate AR terms.

Model Selection: Once candidate orders ($p$, $q$) are identified, select the final model using:

  • $AIC = -2\ln\hat{L} + 2(p+q)$ — minimise
  • $BIC = -2\ln\hat{L} + (p+q)\ln T$ — minimise (more parsimonious)

The selected model is validated by verifying residual white noise via the Ljung-Box test and residual ACF/PACF plot.

[Q.E.D.]

UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (b): Spectral Density Function and Fourier Transform of ACF + AR(1) Spectrum

Reference: Brockwell & Davis, Introduction to Time Series and Forecasting, 3rd ed., Ch. 4; Box, Jenkins, Reinsel & Ljung, Time Series Analysis, 5th ed., Ch. 3; Chatfield, C., The Analysis of Time Series, 6th ed., Ch. 7.

Part (i): Spectral Density Function

Definition: For a weakly stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ with absolutely summable autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$ (i.e., $\sum_{k=-\infty}^\infty |\gamma(k)| < \infty$), the spectral density function (SDF) $f(\omega)$ is defined as the Fourier transform of the autocovariance sequence:

$$f(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\gamma(k)\,e^{-i\omega k}, \quad -\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi \tag{1}$$

Properties of $f(\omega)$:

  • $f(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$ (non-negative)
  • $f(\omega) = f(-\omega)$ (symmetric for real processes)
  • $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(\omega)\,d\omega = \gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t)$ (total power)

The inverse relationship (spectral representation of autocovariance):

$$\gamma(k) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{i\omega k}\,f(\omega)\,d\omega \tag{2}$$

Normalised Spectral Density Function $h(\omega)$: Dividing (1) by $\gamma(0)$:

$$h(\omega) = \frac{f(\omega)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)}\,e^{-i\omega k}$$

Proof that $h(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of the ACF:

The ACF is $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$. Substituting:

$$h(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\rho(k)\,e^{-i\omega k}$$

This is precisely the definition of the (discrete-time) Fourier transform of the sequence $\{\rho(k)\}_{k=-\infty}^\infty$, evaluated at frequency $\omega$.

The inverse: $\rho(k) = \int_{-\pi}^\pi e^{i\omega k}\,h(\omega)\,d\omega$ (Fourier inversion formula). Thus $h(\omega)$ and $\rho(k)$ form a Fourier pair. ✓

Part (ii): Spectral Density Function of an AR(1) Process

The AR(1) process: $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + e_t$, $|\phi| < 1$, $e_t \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma^2)$.

Known autocovariance: $\gamma(k) = \phi^{|k|}\,\frac{\sigma^2}{1-\phi^2}$, $k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$

Substituting into the spectral density formula (1):

$$f(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{\sigma^2}{1-\phi^2}\phi^{|k|}\,e^{-i\omega k} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1-\phi^2)}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\phi^{|k|}e^{-i\omega k}$$

Split the sum at $k=0$:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}\phi^{|k|}e^{-i\omega k} = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\phi^k e^{-i\omega k} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\phi^k e^{i\omega k} = 1 + \frac{\phi e^{-i\omega}}{1-\phi e^{-i\omega}} + \frac{\phi e^{i\omega}}{1-\phi e^{i\omega}}$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$= \frac{(1-\phi e^{-i\omega})(1-\phi e^{i\omega}) + \phi e^{-i\omega}(1-\phi e^{i\omega}) + \phi e^{i\omega}(1-\phi e^{-i\omega})}{(1-\phi e^{-i\omega})(1-\phi e^{i\omega})}$$

Numerator $= 1 - \phi e^{i\omega} - \phi e^{-i\omega} + \phi^2 + \phi e^{-i\omega} - \phi^2 + \phi e^{i\omega} - \phi^2 = 1-\phi^2$

Denominator $= 1 - \phi(e^{i\omega}+e^{-i\omega}) + \phi^2 = 1 - 2\phi\cos\omega + \phi^2$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1 - 2\phi\cos\omega + \phi^2)}, \quad -\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi}$$

Interpretation:

  • For $\phi > 0$ (positive autocorrelation): $f(\omega)$ peaks at $\omega = 0$ (low frequencies dominate — slowly evolving series).
  • For $\phi < 0$ (negative autocorrelation): $f(\omega)$ peaks at $\omega = \pi$ (high frequencies dominate — rapidly oscillating series).
  • As $\phi \to 1$: the spectrum becomes increasingly concentrated at $\omega = 0$ — near-unit-root behaviour.
