2017.5.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2017) — Model Solution


Part (a): Time Path of Price for Dynamic Equilibrium

Reference: Chiang, A. C., Fundamental Methods of Mathematical Economics, 4th ed., Ch. 14 (Differential Equations); Henderson & Quandt, Microeconomic Theory, 3rd ed., Ch. 3.

1. Setting Up the Differential Equation

The demand and supply functions are given as:

Demand: $x_t = 240 + 10\dfrac{dp}{dt}$ (quantity demanded increases when price is rising — speculative demand)

Supply: $x_t = 6p_t - 60$ (quantity supplied increases with price; supply positive when $p > 10$)

Dynamic equilibrium condition: $x_d = x_s$ at all times:

$$240 + 10\frac{dp}{dt} = 6p - 60$$ $$10\frac{dp}{dt} = 6p - 300$$ $$\frac{dp}{dt} - 0.6p = -30 \tag{*}$$

2. Solving the Linear First-Order ODE (*)

This is a first-order linear ODE: $\dfrac{dp}{dt} + a(t)p = g(t)$ with $a = -0.6$ (constant), $g = -30$ (constant).

Particular (Equilibrium) Solution: At static equilibrium $dp/dt = 0$:

$$0 = 6p^* - 300 \implies p^* = 50 \text{ (equilibrium price)}$$

Homogeneous Solution: $\dfrac{dp}{dt} = 0.6p \implies p_h = Ce^{0.6t}$

General Solution:

$$p(t) = p^* + Ce^{0.6t} = 50 + Ce^{0.6t}$$

3. Applying the Initial Condition

At $t = 0$, $p(0) = 72$:

$$72 = 50 + C \implies C = 22$$ $$\boxed{p(t) = 50 + 22e^{0.6t}}$$

4. Interpretation of the Time Path

Equilibrium price: $p^* = 50$ per kg.

Dynamic stability: The solution $p(t) = 50 + 22e^{0.6t}$ diverges from equilibrium as $t \to \infty$ (since $e^{0.6t} \to \infty$). The equilibrium is dynamically unstable.

Economic explanation: The demand function has a positive price-change effect ($+10\,dp/dt$): when prices are rising ($dp/dt > 0$), demand increases further (speculative motive — buyers rush to purchase before prices rise more). This reinforces the upward price movement, creating a self-fulfilling price spiral. Starting at $p_0 = 72 > p^* = 50$, prices accelerate upward without bound. This is typical of speculative commodity bubbles.

Stable case: If demand were $x_d = 240 - 10\,dp/dt$ (anti-speculative), the sign of the exponential would reverse to $e^{-0.6t}$, giving a stable convergent path to $p^* = 50$.

[Q.E.D.]
2017.5.2:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2017) — Model Solution


Part (b): Methods of Computing Price Index Numbers

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; Allen, R. G. D., Index Numbers in Theory and Practice; Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers (1922).

(i) Simple Average of Price Relatives

Compute the price relative for each commodity: $R_i = (p_{1i}/p_{0i}) \times 100$, where $p_{0i}$ and $p_{1i}$ are base and current period prices of commodity $i$.

Simple arithmetic mean:

$$P_{01}^{AM} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n R_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{p_{1i}}{p_{0i}}\times 100$$

Simple geometric mean (preferred for index numbers):

$$P_{01}^{GM} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^n R_i\right)^{1/n} = \text{antilog}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \log R_i\right)$$

Merits: Simple to compute; not influenced by the absolute level of prices; the geometric mean satisfies the time reversal test. Demerits: Gives equal importance to all commodities regardless of their economic significance; arithmetic mean is biased upward.

(ii) Simple Aggregate of Prices

$$P_{01}^{SA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{0i}} \times 100$$

Merits: Extremely simple to compute. Demerits: Strongly influenced by the absolute price levels and units of measurement — a commodity with a high price (e.g., petrol in litres vs. in gallons) dominates the index regardless of economic importance. It does not account for quantities or weights. This method is generally considered unsatisfactory for serious index number construction.

(iii) Weighted Aggregate of Prices

Assign weights $w_i$ reflecting the economic importance of each commodity:

$$P_{01}^{WA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n w_i p_{1i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n w_i p_{0i}} \times 100$$

Choice of weights distinguishes the major indices:

  • Laspeyres Index ($L_{01}$): $w_i = q_{0i}$ (base period quantities): $L_{01} = \dfrac{\sum p_{1i}q_{0i}}{\sum p_{0i}q_{0i}}\times 100$
  • Paasche Index ($P_{01}$): $w_i = q_{1i}$ (current period quantities): $P_{01} = \dfrac{\sum p_{1i}q_{1i}}{\sum p_{0i}q_{1i}}\times 100$
  • Fisher's Ideal Index: $F_{01} = \sqrt{L_{01}\cdot P_{01}}$ (geometric mean of Laspeyres and Paasche)
  • Marshall-Edgeworth Index: $w_i = (q_{0i}+q_{1i})/2$ (average of base and current quantities)

Merits: Reflects economic importance through weights; suitable for general and cost-of-living indices. Demerits: Weight selection affects the index value; Laspeyres overweights relatively expensive base-period goods (upward bias); Paasche is costly to compute (requires current-period quantity data every time).

[Q.E.D.]
2017.5.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2017) — Model Solution


Part (c): Engel Curve Forms and Complications in Family Budget Data

Reference: Koutsoyiannis, A., Modern Microeconomics, 2nd ed., Ch. 2; Stone, R. (1954), "Linear Expenditure Systems and Demand Analysis"; Prais & Houthakker, The Analysis of Family Budgets (1955).

1. Definition and Motivation

An Engel curve relates the expenditure (or quantity consumed) of a commodity to the consumer's income, holding prices constant. Estimated from cross-sectional family budget data, it shows how demand for a good changes as income increases.

2. Common Functional Forms of Engel Curves

FormEquationIncome Elasticity ($\eta$)Suitable for
Linear$E = \alpha + \beta I$$\eta = \beta I/E$ (variable)Normal goods over a moderate income range
Log-linear (Double log)$\ln E = \alpha + \beta\ln I$$\eta = \beta$ (constant)Luxury goods ($\beta>1$), necessities ($\beta<1$)
Semi-log$E = \alpha + \beta\ln I$$\eta = \beta/E$ (variable)Goods with saturation — diminishing response
Hyperbolic (Tornqvist)$E = \alpha + \beta/I$$\eta = -\beta/E<0$Inferior goods (Giffen)
Working-Leser$w = \alpha + \beta\ln I$ ($w$ = budget share)$\eta = 1 + \beta/w$Complete demand systems (budget shares)
Quadratic (Polynomial)$E = \alpha + \beta I + \gamma I^2$VariableGoods with non-monotone income response

3. Complication (i): Household Expenditure Depends on Both Income and Family Size

A single-equation Engel curve omits family size as a determinant of expenditure. Families with more members spend more on food, clothing, etc. even at the same income level.

Solution — Multiple Regression Engel Curve: Include household size $n$ as an additional regressor:

$$E = f(I, n) = \alpha + \beta_1 I + \beta_2 n + u$$

or a more general interaction form:

$$\ln E = \alpha + \beta_1\ln I + \beta_2\ln n + u$$

This allows an equivalence scale — how many additional income units compensate one additional person in the household (the "cost of a child"). Prais & Houthakker (1955) used this approach systematically to derive household equivalence scales from family budget surveys.

4. Complication (ii): Variation in Consumption Due to Age and Sex Composition

Even families of the same size and income level consume differently because of varying age and sex composition — children consume less food but more certain services; elderly members have different health expenditures; males and females differ in clothing expenditure.

Solution — Consumer Unit Approach:

  1. Convert each household to an equivalent number of "adult male equivalents" (consumer units) by assigning weights: e.g., adult male = 1.00, adult female = 0.90, child 10–14 = 0.80, child under 5 = 0.50, etc.
  2. Express household expenditure per consumer unit: $e = E/c$ where $c = $ total consumer units in the household.
  3. Fit the Engel curve: $e = f(I/c)$ — expenditure per consumer unit as a function of income per consumer unit.

The consumer unit weights can themselves be estimated jointly with the Engel curve parameters using maximum likelihood methods (full information approach of Prais & Houthakker).

[Q.E.D.]

Part (c): Gini Coefficient and Lorenz Curve from Decile Income Distribution Data

Reference: Atkinson, A. B., The Economics of Inequality; Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; Gastwirth, J. L. (1971), "A General Definition of the Lorenz Curve", Econometrica.

1. Definition of the Gini Coefficient

The Gini coefficient $G$ measures income inequality. It equals twice the area between the line of perfect equality ($L=p$) and the Lorenz curve $L(p)$:

$$G = 1 - 2\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp \in [0,1]$$

$G=0$: perfect equality; $G=1$: extreme inequality.

2. Given Data and Cumulative Income Shares

DecileRural % ShareUrban % ShareCumulative RuralCumulative Urban
0–103.02.33.02.3
10–204.43.37.45.6
20–305.44.112.89.7
30–406.44.819.214.5
40–507.45.826.620.3
50–608.37.034.927.3
60–709.68.744.536.0
70–8010.910.055.446.0
80–9013.814.069.260.0
90–10030.840.0100.0100.0

3. Gini Coefficient — Trapezoidal Rule

With $n=10$ deciles, each width $= 1/10$, and denoting cumulative proportions $L_0=0, L_1,\ldots, L_{10}=1$ (in [0,1] scale):

$$\text{Area under Lorenz} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{k=1}^{10}\frac{L_{k-1}+L_k}{2} = \frac{1}{20}\sum_{k=0}^{10}L_k - \frac{1}{20}(L_0+L_{10})/2$$

Rural ($L_k/100$): 0, 0.030, 0.074, 0.128, 0.192, 0.266, 0.349, 0.445, 0.554, 0.692, 1.000

Sum of all $L_k$ = 0+0.030+0.074+0.128+0.192+0.266+0.349+0.445+0.554+0.692+1.000 = 3.730

Area = $\frac{3.730 - (0+1)/2 - (0+1)/2 + 1}{20}$... Actually simpler: sum of trapezoid midpoints:

Trapezoid areas (width=1/10): $\sum_{k=1}^{10}(L_{k-1}+L_k)/2 \times (1/10) = \frac{1}{20}\sum_{k=0}^{10}L_k - \frac{L_0+L_{10}}{20}$

= $(3.730 - 0.5)/20 = 3.230/20$... Let me use: Area = $(1/10) \times \frac{0+3.0+7.4+12.8+19.2+26.6+34.9+44.5+55.4+69.2+100}{2 \times 100}$

= $(1/10) \times (373.0 - 50)/100 = (1/10) \times 0.323$...

Simpler: Area = $\frac{1}{10}(0.030+0.074+0.128+0.192+0.266+0.349+0.445+0.554+0.692+1.000) - \frac{1}{20}(1.000)$

= $\frac{3.730}{10} - \frac{1.0}{20} = 0.3730 - 0.0500 = 0.3230$

$G_{Rural} = 1 - 2 \times 0.3230 = \mathbf{0.354}$

Urban ($L_k/100$): 0, 0.023, 0.056, 0.097, 0.145, 0.203, 0.273, 0.360, 0.460, 0.600, 1.000; Sum = 3.217

Area = $3.217/10 - 1/20 = 0.3217 - 0.0500 = 0.2717$

$G_{Urban} = 1 - 2 \times 0.2717 = \mathbf{0.457}$

4. Interpretation

  • Rural Gini (0.354): Moderate inequality — bottom 50% receive only 26.6% of income
  • Urban Gini (0.457): Higher inequality — bottom 50% receive only 20.3% of income; top 10% alone receive 40%
  • Urban areas have substantially higher income inequality than rural areas

Lorenz Curve construction: Plot cumulative population (x-axis: 0,10,...,100%) against cumulative income (y-axis: corresponding cumulative shares). The line of perfect equality is the 45° diagonal. The Lorenz curves for both rural and urban sectors lie below this diagonal, with the urban curve bowing further downward, confirming higher inequality.

UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2019) — Model Solution


Part (c): Equilibrium Prices and Supplies for Three Commodities

Reference: Henderson & Quandt, Microeconomic Theory, 3rd ed., Ch. 2; Koutsoyiannis, A., Modern Microeconomics, 2nd ed., Ch. 3.

1. Setting Up the Equilibrium System

At equilibrium, demand = supply for each commodity: $d_i = S_i$.

Given functions:

  • $d_A = 64-9p_A-3p_B-3p_C$, $S_A = 10+6p_A$
  • $d_B = 12+3p_A-6p_B+3p_C$, $S_B = 15+6p_B$
  • $d_C = 21-3p_A+3p_B-5p_C$, $S_C = 3+p_C$

2. Equilibrium Equations

$d_A = S_A$: $64-9p_A-3p_B-3p_C = 10+6p_A \Rightarrow 5p_A+p_B+p_C = 18 \;\;(1)$

$d_B = S_B$: $12+3p_A-6p_B+3p_C = 15+6p_B \Rightarrow p_A-4p_B+p_C = 1 \;\;(2)$

$d_C = S_C$: $21-3p_A+3p_B-5p_C = 3+p_C \Rightarrow p_A-p_B+2p_C = 6 \;\;(3)$

3. Solving the System

(1)−(2): $4p_A+5p_B = 17 \;\;(4)$

(3)−(2): $3p_B+p_C = 5 \Rightarrow p_C = 5-3p_B \;\;(5)$

Substitute (5) into (2): $p_A-4p_B+(5-3p_B) = 1 \Rightarrow p_A-7p_B = -4 \;\;(6)$

From (4): $p_A = (17-5p_B)/4$. Substitute into (6):

$$\frac{17-5p_B}{4}-7p_B = -4 \Rightarrow 17-5p_B-28p_B = -16 \Rightarrow 33p_B = 33 \Rightarrow \boxed{p_B = 1}$$ $$p_A = (17-5)/4 = 3 \Rightarrow \boxed{p_A = 3}, \quad p_C = 5-3(1) = 2 \Rightarrow \boxed{p_C = 2}$$

4. Equilibrium Supplies

$$S_A = 10+6(3) = \mathbf{28}, \quad S_B = 15+6(1) = \mathbf{21}, \quad S_C = 3+2 = \mathbf{5}$$

5. Verification

$d_A = 64-9(3)-3(1)-3(2) = 64-27-3-6 = 28 = S_A$ ✓

$d_B = 12+3(3)-6(1)+3(2) = 12+9-6+6 = 21 = S_B$ ✓

$d_C = 21-3(3)+3(1)-5(2) = 21-9+3-10 = 5 = S_C$ ✓

[Q.E.D.]
2019.5.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2019) — Model Solution


Part (a): Algebraic Relationships Among Index Numbers

Reference: Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers (1922); Allen, R. G. D., Index Numbers in Theory and Practice.

Part (i): Given Any Three of L(p), P(p), L(q), P(q), Find the Fourth

Define (base period 0, current period 1): $V_{01} = \sum p_1 q_1/\sum p_0 q_0$ (value ratio).

Key cross-multiplication identities:

$$L(p) \times P(q) = \frac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times \frac{\sum q_1 p_1}{\sum q_0 p_1} = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0} = V_{01}$$ $$P(p) \times L(q) = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_1} \times \frac{\sum q_1 p_0}{\sum q_0 p_0} = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0} = V_{01}$$

Therefore: $L(p)\cdot P(q) = P(p)\cdot L(q) = V_{01}$. This gives:

$$\frac{L(p)}{P(p)} = \frac{L(q)}{P(q)}$$

So if any three of $\{L(p), P(p), L(q), P(q)\}$ are known, the fourth is uniquely determined from this identity. For example, if $L(p), P(p), L(q)$ are known, then $P(q) = L(q)\cdot P(p)/L(p)$. ✓

Part (ii): L and P from Bowley's Index B and Fisher's Index F

Bowley's (arithmetic mean) index: $B = (L+P)/2$, i.e., $L+P = 2B$.

Fisher's ideal index: $F = \sqrt{LP}$, i.e., $LP = F^2$.

Given $B = x$ and $F = y$: $L$ and $P$ are the two roots of the quadratic:

$$t^2 - (L+P)t + LP = 0 \implies t^2 - 2xt + y^2 = 0$$ $$t = \frac{2x \pm \sqrt{4x^2 - 4y^2}}{2} = x \pm \sqrt{x^2-y^2}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{L = x + \sqrt{x^2-y^2}, \; P = x - \sqrt{x^2-y^2}} \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{L = x - \sqrt{x^2-y^2}, \; P = x + \sqrt{x^2-y^2}}$$

Since Laspeyres $L \geq$ Fisher $F \geq$ Paasche $P$ (by the Bortkiewicz inequality), and $x = B = (L+P)/2$, we have $L \geq x \geq P$. The first assignment ($L = x+\sqrt{x^2-y^2}, P = x-\sqrt{x^2-y^2}$) is the appropriate one since $L \geq P$ requires taking the larger root for $L$. The condition $x^2 \geq y^2$, i.e., $B^2 \geq F^2$, is guaranteed since $B = (L+P)/2 \geq \sqrt{LP} = F$ by the AM-GM inequality. ✓

[Q.E.D.]
2019.5.2:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2019) — Model Solution


Part (b): Fixed Base vs. Chain Base Index Numbers

1. Definitions and Fundamental Distinction

Fixed Base Index Number (FBI): An index number in which a single base period, selected once and kept constant throughout the entire series, is used as the reference point for all comparisons. If period 0 is the fixed base, then the index for period $t$ is:

$$P_t = \frac{p_t}{p_0} \times 100$$

All temporal comparisons are thus made against the same period $p_0$.

Chain Base Index Number (CBI): An index number in which the immediately preceding period serves as the base for each successive period. The index for period $t$, called the link relative, is:

$$L_t = \frac{p_t}{p_{t-1}} \times 100$$

Long-run comparisons are made by chaining (multiplying sequentially) these link relatives.

2. Relative Merits and Demerits

FeatureFixed BaseChain Base
Stability of comparison All comparisons anchored to one period — consistent. Each period compared to its predecessor — less stable over the long run.
Updating flexibility Adding new commodities requires revision from the base year onwards — cumbersome. New commodities and weights can be incorporated at any link — highly flexible.
Base obsolescence If the base year is abnormal (war, pandemic), comparisons become misleading over time. Never suffers from an outdated base; each link reflects current conditions.
Long-period accuracy Reliable for long-period trend measurement (no chaining error). Chaining errors may accumulate over many links.
Computational effort Straightforward once weights and base prices are fixed. Requires sequential multiplication — slightly more involved but manageable.
Practical use CSO Consumer Price Index (CPI), WPI use fixed base updated periodically. EU Harmonised Index, OECD chain-linking methods.

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics, Part II; Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers.

3. Conversion Formula: Chain Base → Fixed Base

Let the chain base index for year $t$ be $L_t$ (link relative) and let the fixed base index for year $t$ be $F_t$ (base year = 100). The conversion relationship is:

$$F_t = \frac{F_{t-1} \times L_t}{100}$$

We start by setting $F_{\text{base}} = 100$ for the chosen fixed base year.

4. Numerical Calculation

Given data (Chain Base Index Numbers):

Year2005200620072008200920102011
CBI ($L_t$)9810211513512510395

Fixed Base: 2005 = 100. (The $L_{2005} = 98$ implies 2005 is at 98 relative to 2004. We adopt 2005 as our reference, so $F_{2005} = 100$ by convention.)

  • $F_{2005} = 100.00$ (fixed base)
  • $F_{2006} = \dfrac{100 \times 102}{100} = 102.00$
  • $F_{2007} = \dfrac{102 \times 115}{100} = 117.30$
  • $F_{2008} = \dfrac{117.30 \times 135}{100} = \dfrac{15835.5}{100} = 158.36$ (rounded to 2 d.p.)
  • $F_{2009} = \dfrac{158.36 \times 125}{100} = \dfrac{19794.9}{100} = 197.95$
  • $F_{2010} = \dfrac{197.95 \times 103}{100} = \dfrac{20388.9}{100} = 203.89$
  • $F_{2011} = \dfrac{203.89 \times 95}{100} = \dfrac{19369.5}{100} = 193.70$

Summary Table (Fixed Base, 2005 = 100):

2020.8.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2020) — Model Solution


Part (a): Pareto's Law of Income Distribution and Bounded Income PDF

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics, 4th ed.; Kendall, Ord & Stuart, The Advanced Theory of Statistics, Vol. 1, Ch. 6; Atkinson, A. B., The Economics of Inequality.

1. Pareto's Law of Income Distribution

Pareto (1897) proposed that the number of individuals $N(x)$ with income at least $x$ in any society follows:

$$N(x) = A x^{-\alpha}, \quad x \geq x_0, \quad \alpha > 0, \quad A > 0$$

where $\alpha$ is the Pareto exponent (typically $1 < \alpha < 3$ for empirical income distributions). A larger $\alpha$ implies a more equal distribution.

Pareto's law is an empirical regularity observed across diverse economies and time periods: the upper tail of the income distribution is well-described by a power law. The law is sometimes stated as: the log of the number of individuals receiving an income above $x$ is approximately a linear decreasing function of $\log(x)$:

$$\log N(x) = \log A - \alpha \log x$$

2. Probability Density Function for Bounded Income $a \leq X \leq b$

In a finite population, incomes lie in the range $[a, b]$ where $a$ is the minimum income and $b$ is the maximum income. The total number of individuals with income in $[a, b]$ is:

$$N_{total} = N(a) - N(b) = A a^{-\alpha} - A b^{-\alpha} = A(a^{-\alpha} - b^{-\alpha})$$

The number with income in $[x, x+dx]$ is $-dN = \alpha A x^{-(\alpha+1)}dx$ (since $N(x)$ is decreasing in $x$).

The probability density function of income $X$ over the bounded range $[a, b]$ is therefore:

$$f(x) = \frac{-dN/dx}{N_{total}} = \frac{\alpha A x^{-(\alpha+1)}}{A(a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha})} = \frac{\alpha x^{-(\alpha+1)}}{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}}, \quad a \leq x \leq b$$

This can also be written as:

$$\boxed{f(x) = \frac{\alpha}{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}} \cdot x^{-(\alpha+1)}, \quad a \leq x \leq b}$$

3. Verification

$$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \frac{\alpha}{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}}\int_a^b x^{-(\alpha+1)}\,dx = \frac{\alpha}{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}}\left[\frac{-x^{-\alpha}}{\alpha}\right]_a^b = \frac{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}}{a^{-\alpha}-b^{-\alpha}} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

4. Special Case: Unbounded Pareto ($b \to \infty$)

As $b \to \infty$: $b^{-\alpha} \to 0$, so $f(x) \to \alpha a^\alpha x^{-(\alpha+1)}$ — the standard Pareto Type I pdf:

$$f(x) = \alpha a^\alpha x^{-(\alpha+1)}, \quad x \geq a \quad (\text{requires } \alpha > 1 \text{ for finite mean})$$ [Q.E.D.]

2021.4.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2021) — Model Solution


Part (a): Joint PDF — Regression of $Y$ on $X$ Linear, $X$ on $Y$ Not Linear

Reference: Hogg, McKean & Craig, Introduction to Mathematical Statistics, 8th ed., Ch. 2; Mood, Graybill & Boes, Introduction to the Theory of Statistics, 3rd ed., Ch. 4.

1. Setup

Joint pdf: $f(x,y)=1/x$ for $|y|

2. Marginal Distributions

$$f_X(x) = \int_{-x}^{x}\frac{1}{x}\,dy = \frac{1}{x}\cdot 2x = 2, \quad 0So $X\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ (with pdf 2? — actually $X\sim\text{Beta}(1,1)$ scaled: $f_X(x)=2$ is a $\text{Beta}(2,1)$ distribution — here $f_X(x)=2$ on $(0,1)$ is correct).

$$f_Y(y) = \int_{|y|}^{1}\frac{1}{x}\,dx = [-\ln x]_{|y|}^{1} = -\ln|y|, \quad 0<|y|<1$$

3. Conditional Distributions

$$f(y|x) = \frac{f(x,y)}{f_X(x)} = \frac{1/x}{2} = \frac{1}{2x}, \quad |y|This is $\text{Uniform}(-x,x)$.

$$f(x|y) = \frac{f(x,y)}{f_Y(y)} = \frac{1/x}{-\ln|y|}, \quad |y|4. Regression of $Y$ on $X$ — Linear $$E(Y|X=x) = \int_{-x}^{x}y\cdot\frac{1}{2x}\,dy = \frac{1}{2x}\cdot\left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{-x}^{x} = \frac{1}{2x}\cdot 0 = 0$$

Since $E(Y|X=x) = 0 = \alpha + \beta x$ (with $\alpha=\beta=0$), the regression of $Y$ on $X$ is linear. ✓

5. Regression of $X$ on $Y$ — Not Linear

$$E(X|Y=y) = \int_{|y|}^{1}x\cdot\frac{1/x}{-\ln|y|}\,dx = \frac{1}{-\ln|y|}\int_{|y|}^{1}1\,dx = \frac{1-|y|}{-\ln|y|}$$

This is a function of $|y|$, not a linear function of $y$. To confirm non-linearity: $E(X|Y=0)=\lim_{y\to0}\frac{1-|y|}{-\ln|y|}=\frac{1}{\infty}=0$ and $E(X|Y=0.5)=\frac{0.5}{-\ln0.5}=\frac{0.5}{0.693}\approx0.72$ and $E(X|Y=0.9)=\frac{0.1}{0.105}\approx0.95$. A linear fit through these points does not pass through all of them (the function grows faster than linear near $|y|=1$). Therefore the regression of $X$ on $Y$ is not linear. ✓

[Q.E.D.]
2021.5.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2021) — Model Solution


Part (a): Laspeyres-Paasche Identity: $L(p)/L(q) = P(p)/P(q)$

Reference: Bowley, A.L. (1923), The Nature and Purpose of the Measurement of Social Phenomena; Allen, R.G.D. (1975), Index Numbers in Theory and Practice, Ch. 2; Fisher, I. (1922), The Making of Index Numbers.

1. Definitions

Let subscript 0 denote base period and 1 denote current period. $p$: price, $q$: quantity.

$$L(p) = \frac{\sum_i p_{1i}q_{0i}}{\sum_i p_{0i}q_{0i}} \quad (\text{Laspeyres price index})$$ $$P(p) = \frac{\sum_i p_{1i}q_{1i}}{\sum_i p_{0i}q_{1i}} \quad (\text{Paasche price index})$$ $$L(q) = \frac{\sum_i q_{1i}p_{0i}}{\sum_i q_{0i}p_{0i}} = \frac{\sum_i p_{0i}q_{1i}}{\sum_i p_{0i}q_{0i}} \quad (\text{Laspeyres quantity index})$$ $$P(q) = \frac{\sum_i q_{1i}p_{1i}}{\sum_i q_{0i}p_{1i}} = \frac{\sum_i p_{1i}q_{1i}}{\sum_i p_{1i}q_{0i}} \quad (\text{Paasche quantity index})$$

2. Proof of $L(p)/L(q) = P(p)/P(q)$

Denote $V_0=\sum_i p_{0i}q_{0i}$, $V_1=\sum_i p_{1i}q_{1i}$, $V_{10}=\sum_i p_{1i}q_{0i}$, $V_{01}=\sum_i p_{0i}q_{1i}$.

$$L(p) = \frac{V_{10}}{V_0}, \quad L(q) = \frac{V_{01}}{V_0}, \quad P(p) = \frac{V_1}{V_{01}}, \quad P(q) = \frac{V_1}{V_{10}}$$ $$\frac{L(p)}{L(q)} = \frac{V_{10}/V_0}{V_{01}/V_0} = \frac{V_{10}}{V_{01}}$$ $$\frac{P(p)}{P(q)} = \frac{V_1/V_{01}}{V_1/V_{10}} = \frac{V_{10}}{V_{01}}$$ $$\therefore \quad \boxed{\frac{L(p)}{L(q)} = \frac{P(p)}{P(q)} = \frac{\sum_i p_{1i}q_{0i}}{\sum_i p_{0i}q_{1i}}}$$

Economic Interpretation: The common ratio $V_{10}/V_{01}$ is the ratio of current-period expenditure evaluated at base quantities to base-period expenditure evaluated at current quantities. It measures the relative price-change independently of the quantity-change weighting scheme.

[Q.E.D.]

2022.1.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (c): Consumer Price Index — Construction Steps and Precautions

Reference: CSO, Manual on Consumer Price Index, 2018; ILO/IMF/OECD, Consumer Price Index Manual: Theory and Practice, 2004; Bowley, A.L., Elements of Statistics.

Part (i): Steps in Constructing a Consumer Price Index (CPI)

A Consumer Price Index measures changes over time in the general level of prices of goods and services that a reference population acquires for the purpose of consumption. The main steps are:

Step 1: Define the Reference Population and Scope

Specify the population group to be covered (e.g., urban wage earners, industrial workers, rural agricultural labourers). In India, CSO publishes CPI (Combined) for rural, urban, and all-India separately since 2012 (base 2012=100).

Step 2: Conduct Household Expenditure Survey

Carry out a consumer expenditure survey (e.g., NSS CES in India) to determine which commodities are consumed and their expenditure shares. This survey provides the commodity basket and weights.

Step 3: Select Commodities and Determine Weights

Group commodities into major groups (India's CPI groups: Food & Beverages, Pan/Tobacco, Clothing, Housing, Fuel & Light, Miscellaneous). Weights $W_i$ are the percentage expenditure shares of each commodity/group in total consumer expenditure from the survey:

$$W_i = \frac{p_{0i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_j p_{0j}\,q_{0j}} \times 100$$
Step 4: Select Price Collection Centres

Choose representative markets/outlets across the geographic coverage. In India, Labour Bureau collects prices from pre-selected markets in selected cities/towns at fixed intervals (weekly for food items, monthly for others).

Step 5: Collect Prices Periodically

Collect retail transaction prices (not list/wholesale prices) at regular intervals from selected outlets. Prices should be for standard, well-defined quality specifications to ensure comparability over time.

Step 6: Compute Item-Level Price Relatives

For each commodity $i$, compute the price relative:

$$R_i = \frac{p_{1i}}{p_{0i}} \times 100$$

For elementary aggregates within an item, use geometric mean of price relatives (Jevons approach) or arithmetic mean (Dutot approach) as per the ILO Manual recommendations.

Step 7: Aggregate to Group and All-Item Indices

The CPI (Family Budget / Laspeyres method) is computed as the weighted arithmetic mean of price relatives:

$$CPI = \sum_{i=1}^n W_i R_i = \frac{\sum_i p_{1i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_i p_{0i}\,q_{0i}} \times 100$$

This is the Laspeyres price index (as proved formally in the COLI equivalence).

Step 8: Link and Chain (for Base Year Revision)

When the base year is revised, link the old and new series using a splicing factor to maintain continuity.

Part (ii): Precautions While Using the CPI

  1. Base Year Relevance: The base year commodity basket must be periodically revised. An outdated basket (e.g., India's old WPI base of 2004–05) does not reflect current consumption patterns. Check when the CPI base was last revised.
  2. Quality Change Bias: If product quality improves while the price remains constant, a pure price change is overstated. Hedonic adjustments (common in CPIs of developed countries) are used to correct this. Ignoring quality change leads to upward bias in CPI.
  3. New Goods Bias: New products (e.g., smartphones, OTT services) are typically not included immediately in the basket. Their omission causes the CPI to overstate inflation.
  4. Substitution Bias: The Laspeyres CPI uses fixed base-year weights and therefore overstates the cost of living (it ignores consumer substitution away from goods that become relatively expensive). The true cost-of-living index lies between the Laspeyres and Paasche.
  5. Outlet Substitution: Consumers shift purchases to cheaper outlets (discount stores, e-commerce). If only traditional outlets are priced, the CPI overstates inflation.
  6. Regional Variation: A national CPI hides regional price differences. It should not be used to compare purchasing power across regions without appropriate adjustments.
  7. Income Group Variation: Different income groups have different commodity baskets. A single CPI may not accurately reflect inflation experienced by the very poor or the very rich. India publishes separate CPI-IW, CPI-AL, CPI-RL for different groups.
  8. Not a Deflator for All Purposes: CPI measures consumer prices; it is not appropriate for deflating GDP or industrial production. Use GDP deflator or WPI for those purposes.
[Q.E.D.]
2022.5.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (a): Concentration Curves under Pareto's Income Distribution

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics, 4th ed.; Atkinson, A. B., The Economics of Inequality; Kendall, Ord & Stuart, The Advanced Theory of Statistics, Vol. 1, Ch. 6; Gastwirth, J. L. (1971), "A General Definition of the Lorenz Curve", Econometrica.

1. The Pareto Distribution — Recap

Pareto's law states that the number of income recipients with income $\geq x$ is:

$$N(x) = A x^{-\alpha}, \quad x \geq x_0, \quad \alpha > 1$$

The Pareto pdf (for one individual drawn at random): $f(x) = \alpha x_0^\alpha x^{-(\alpha+1)}$, $x \geq x_0$.

2. Concentration Curves (Lorenz Curves)

A concentration curve (also called the Lorenz Curve in income inequality analysis) is a graphical device that plots the cumulative share of total income (y-axis, $q$-axis) against the cumulative share of population (x-axis, $p$-axis), where units are ranked from poorest to richest. It was introduced by Lorenz (1905) and formalised by Gastwirth (1971).

Formally, if $F(x)$ is the CDF of income and $\mu$ is the mean income:

$$L(p) = \frac{1}{\mu}\int_{x_0}^{F^{-1}(p)} x\,f(x)\,dx, \quad p \in [0,1]$$

3. Derivation of the Lorenz Curve for the Pareto Distribution

From the Pareto CDF: $F(x) = 1-(x_0/x)^\alpha$, so $F^{-1}(p) = x_0(1-p)^{-1/\alpha}$.

Mean income: $\mu = \alpha x_0/(\alpha-1)$ (requires $\alpha > 1$).

Cumulative income up to the $p$-th quantile:

$$\int_{x_0}^{x_0(1-p)^{-1/\alpha}} x \cdot \alpha x_0^\alpha x^{-(\alpha+1)}\,dx = \frac{\alpha x_0^\alpha}{\alpha-1}\left[x_0^{1-\alpha} - x_0^{1-\alpha}(1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\right] = \mu[1-(1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{L(p) = 1 - (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}}$$

4. Properties of the Lorenz Curve for Pareto

  • $L(0) = 0$, $L(1) = 1$: boundary conditions satisfied. ✓
  • $L(p) \leq p$ for all $p$ (lies below the line of equality): since $(1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha} \geq 1-p$ for $\alpha > 1$, implying $1-(1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha} \leq p$. ✓
  • $L''(p) \geq 0$: the curve is convex — the Lorenz curve always lies below (or on) the line of perfect equality.
  • As $\alpha \to \infty$: $L(p) \to p$ (perfect equality, every income percentile gets its proportionate share).
  • As $\alpha \to 1^+$: $L(p) \to 0$ for $p < 1$ (extreme inequality: all income goes to the top earner).

5. Area Interpretation and Gini Coefficient

The Gini coefficient equals twice the area between the line of equality ($L = p$) and the Lorenz curve:

$$G = 1 - 2\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp = 1 - 2\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2\alpha-1}\right) = \frac{1}{2\alpha-1}$$

Larger $\alpha$ $\Rightarrow$ smaller $G$ $\Rightarrow$ more equal distribution. The parameter $\alpha$ is thus the Pareto inequality index: it is an inverse measure of concentration.

6. Interpretation of the Concentration Curve

Practically, the Lorenz curve (concentration curve) conveys: the poorest $p \times 100\%$ of the population receive only $L(p) \times 100\%$ of total income. For Pareto with $\alpha = 2$:

$$L(0.8) = 1-(0.2)^{1/2} = 1-0.4472 = 0.5528$$

i.e., the bottom 80% of the population earn only 55.3% of total income — the top 20% earns 44.7% (Pareto's 80-20 rule in this case).

[Q.E.D.]
2022.5.2:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (b): Price Elasticity of Demand — Definition and Numerical

Reference: Koutsoyiannis, A., Modern Microeconomics, 2nd ed., Ch. 2; Henderson & Quandt, Microeconomic Theory, 3rd ed.; Allen, R. G. D., Mathematical Analysis for Economists.

1. Definition of Price Elasticity of Demand

The price elasticity of demand $\eta_p$ measures the proportionate change in quantity demanded in response to a proportionate change in price, ceteris paribus. For a demand function $x = g(p)$ (or its inverse $p = h(x)$):

$$\eta_p = \frac{\partial x}{\partial p}\cdot\frac{p}{x}$$

Since the law of demand implies $\partial x/\partial p < 0$, we have $\eta_p < 0$ for normal goods. Often only the absolute value $|\eta_p|$ is cited:

  • $|\eta_p| > 1$: elastic demand — quantity responds more than proportionately to price changes
  • $|\eta_p| = 1$: unit elastic — proportionate changes are equal
  • $|\eta_p| < 1$: inelastic demand — quantity responds less than proportionately

2. Numerical: Demand Function $p = 7 - 8x$

Given: $p = 7 - 8x$ (inverse demand function), where $x$ = quantity demanded, $p$ = price.

Express $x$ as a function of $p$:

$$p = 7 - 8x \implies x = \frac{7-p}{8}$$

Compute $\frac{dx}{dp}$:

$$\frac{dx}{dp} = -\frac{1}{8}$$

Price elasticity formula:

$$\eta_p = \frac{dx}{dp}\cdot\frac{p}{x} = -\frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{p}{x}$$

Set $|\eta_p| = 1$ (unit elasticity):

$$\left|-\frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{p}{x}\right| = 1 \implies \frac{p}{x} = 8 \implies p = 8x \tag{*}$$

Solve simultaneously with the demand function:

Substituting $(*)$ into $p = 7 - 8x$:

$$8x = 7 - 8x \implies 16x = 7 \implies \boxed{x = \frac{7}{16} = 0.4375}$$

Corresponding price:

$$p = 7 - 8\times\frac{7}{16} = 7 - \frac{7}{2} = \frac{7}{2} = \boxed{3.5}$$

Verification:

$$\eta_p = -\frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{3.5}{7/16} = -\frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{3.5 \times 16}{7} = -\frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{56}{7} = -\frac{1}{8}\times 8 = -1$$

$|\eta_p| = 1$. ✓

3. Geometric Interpretation

For a linear demand curve $p = a - bx$ (i.e., $x = (a-p)/b$), the elasticity at any point is:

$$\eta_p = -\frac{1}{b}\cdot\frac{p}{(a-p)/b} = -\frac{p}{a-p}$$

Unit elasticity occurs at $p = a/2$ — the midpoint of the price axis. At $p = 7/2 = 3.5 = a/2$ (with $a=7$), this confirms our answer. Above $p = 3.5$ the demand is elastic; below it is inelastic.

[Q.E.D.]
2022.5.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2022) — Model Solution


Part (c): (i) Role of CSO in Indian Statistical System; (ii) Income Method vs. Expenditure Method

Reference: MOSPI, National Statistical Commission Report (2001); NSSO documents; National Accounts Statistics, CSO/MOSPI; UN System of National Accounts 2008 (SNA 2008).

Part (i): Role of CSO (now MOSPI) in the Indian Statistical System

The Central Statistics Office (CSO), reconstituted as the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MOSPI), is the apex body for statistical coordination and data dissemination in India. Its key functions are:

  1. National Accounts (National Income Compilation): CSO is the sole agency responsible for compiling India's National Accounts Statistics (NAS) — GDP, GNP, NNP, household savings, and capital formation estimates — following the UN SNA 2008 framework. Current base year: 2011–12.
  2. Index of Industrial Production (IIP): CSO compiles and releases the monthly IIP, measuring month-on-month changes in the volume of industrial production across Mining, Manufacturing, and Electricity sectors (base 2011–12).
  3. Consumer Price Index (CPI): Since January 2012, MOSPI releases the combined CPI (rural, urban, all-India) with base 2012=100, replacing the earlier series.
  4. Coordination of Statistical Activities: CSO coordinates the statistical functions of Central Ministries, State Statistical Bureaus (SSBs), and other agencies. It formulates guidelines for data collection, classification, and dissemination.
  5. Census and Survey Supervision: NSSO (National Sample Survey Organisation) — now merged with CSO as NSO (National Statistical Office) — conducts large-scale household surveys on consumption, employment, health, and education. Results inform policy decisions.
  6. Annual Survey of Industries (ASI): Provides detailed data on industrial establishments for use in national accounts, IIP, and input-output tables.
  7. Statistical Standards: Develops and maintains standard definitions, classifications (NIC — National Industrial Classification; NSSO consumption categories), and methodologies in conformity with international standards (ISIC, SNA, BPM6).
  8. International Reporting: Compiles and furnishes statistical data to international organisations (UN, IMF, World Bank, ILO, ADB).
  9. India Statistical Strengthening Project: Capacity-building for state statistical systems to improve the quality, timeliness, and coverage of state-level statistics.

Part (ii): Income Method vs. Expenditure Method of Estimating National Income

FeatureIncome MethodExpenditure Method
Basic concept NI = Sum of all factor incomes paid to factors of production (labour, capital, land, entrepreneurship) NI = Sum of all final expenditures on goods and services produced in the economy
Formula $NI = W + R + I + P$ where $W$ = wages & salaries, $R$ = rent, $I$ = interest, $P$ = profit (undistributed + distributed) $GDP_{MP} = C + I + G + (X-M)$; then $NI = GDP_{MP} - D + NFIA - NIT$
Perspective Production side — factor payments arising from production process Demand side — expenditure by all economic agents on final output
Data sources (India) RBI data on bank profits; EPFO/ESIC for wage data; company balance sheets; agricultural income estimates; self-employment income (NSS) Government budget accounts (G); RBI Balance of Payments (X, M); CSO investment surveys (I); NSS household surveys (C)
Transfer payments Excluded — pensions, subsidies, social security payments are not factor incomes Excluded — only final expenditure on produced goods/services is counted
Problem: double counting Avoided by counting only factor incomes (not intermediate transactions) Avoided by counting only final expenditures (intermediate goods excluded)
Major difficulty Imputed incomes (owner-occupied housing rent, subsistence farming); informal sector income is hard to measure Valuing government services (at cost, not market price); estimating inventory changes
Result obtained NNP at Factor Cost (National Income) GDP at Market Prices (needs adjustment for depreciation, NFIA, net indirect taxes)

Theoretical identity: All three methods (Income, Expenditure, and Output/Value Added) yield the same National Income figure since every rupee of output produced generates an equal amount of income for factors of production, which in turn is spent on goods and services. Any discrepancy in practice is termed a statistical discrepancy.

[Q.E.D.]

2023.3.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2023) — Model Solution


Part (a): Laspeyres, Paasche, and Fisher Index Numbers — Formulation and Tests

Reference: Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers (1922); Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; Allen, R. G. D., Index Numbers in Theory and Practice.

1. Formulation

Let subscript 0 denote the base period and subscript 1 denote the current period. Let $p_{0i}, p_{1i}$ be the prices and $q_{0i}, q_{1i}$ be the quantities of the $i$-th commodity ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$).

Laspeyres Price Index ($L_{01}$): Uses base-period quantities as weights.

$$L_{01} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{0i}\,q_{0i}} \times 100$$

Paasche Price Index ($P_{01}$): Uses current-period quantities as weights.

$$P_{01} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}\,q_{1i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{0i}\,q_{1i}} \times 100$$

Fisher's Ideal Price Index ($F_{01}$): Geometric mean of Laspeyres and Paasche.

$$F_{01} = \sqrt{L_{01} \times P_{01}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum p_{1i}q_{0i}}{\sum p_{0i}q_{0i}} \times \frac{\sum p_{1i}q_{1i}}{\sum p_{0i}q_{1i}}} \times 100$$

Fisher's Ideal Quantity Index ($F^Q_{01}$): Obtained by interchanging $p$ and $q$ roles:

$$F^Q_{01} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum q_{1i}p_{0i}}{\sum q_{0i}p_{0i}} \times \frac{\sum q_{1i}p_{1i}}{\sum q_{0i}p_{1i}}}$$

2. Time Reversal Test (TRT)

Statement: A price index satisfies the TRT if reversing the roles of the base and current periods gives the reciprocal of the original index:

$$P_{01} \times P_{10} = 1$$

Failure of Laspeyres and Paasche:

For Laspeyres: $L_{01} \times L_{10} = \dfrac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times \dfrac{\sum p_0 q_1}{\sum p_1 q_1} \neq 1$ in general (since the quantities weighting the numerator and denominator differ).

Proof that Fisher satisfies TRT:

On reversing the time subscripts (i.e., swapping 0 and 1), the Laspeyres index for period 0 relative to base period 1 is:

$$L_{10} = \frac{\sum p_{0i}\,q_{1i}}{\sum p_{1i}\,q_{1i}} = \frac{1}{P_{01}}$$

(since the numerator and denominator of $P_{01}$ are interchanged exactly)

Similarly, the Paasche index for period 0 relative to base period 1 is:

$$P_{10} = \frac{\sum p_{0i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum p_{1i}\,q_{0i}} = \frac{1}{L_{01}}$$

Therefore the Fisher index $F_{10} = \sqrt{L_{10} \times P_{10}}$:

$$F_{10} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{P_{01}} \times \frac{1}{L_{01}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_{01} \times P_{01}}} = \frac{1}{F_{01}}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{F_{01} \times F_{10} = F_{01} \times \frac{1}{F_{01}} = 1}$$

Fisher's Ideal Index satisfies the Time Reversal Test. ✓

3. Factor Reversal Test (FRT)

Statement: A price index $P_{01}$ and the corresponding quantity index $Q_{01}$ satisfy the FRT if their product equals the ratio of aggregate values (value ratio):

$$P_{01} \times Q_{01} = \frac{\sum p_{1i}\,q_{1i}}{\sum p_{0i}\,q_{0i}} = V_{01}$$

Proof for Fisher's Ideal Index:

Fisher's Price Index:

$$F^P_{01} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_1}}$$

Fisher's Quantity Index (swap $p$ and $q$):

$$F^Q_{01} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum q_1 p_0}{\sum q_0 p_0} \times \frac{\sum q_1 p_1}{\sum q_0 p_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum p_0 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_1 q_0}}$$

Their product:

$$F^P_{01} \times F^Q_{01} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \cdot \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_1} \cdot \frac{\sum p_0 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0} \cdot \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_1 q_0}}$$

Rearranging the four terms inside the square root:

$$= \sqrt{\frac{\sum p_1 q_0 \cdot \sum p_0 q_1 \cdot (\sum p_1 q_1)^2}{\sum p_1 q_0 \cdot \sum p_0 q_1 \cdot (\sum p_0 q_0)^2}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{(\sum p_1 q_1)^2}{(\sum p_0 q_0)^2}} = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0} = V_{01}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{F^P_{01} \times F^Q_{01} = \frac{\sum p_{1i}\,q_{1i}}{\sum p_{0i}\,q_{0i}}}$$

Fisher's Ideal Index satisfies the Factor Reversal Test. ✓

4. Significance

Fisher called his index "ideal" precisely because it is the only index that simultaneously satisfies both TRT and FRT among simple functional forms of Laspeyres and Paasche. Laspeyres fails TRT and FRT; Paasche similarly fails both. Fisher's geometric mean structure creates the exact cancellation that yields these symmetry properties.

[Q.E.D.]
2023.3.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2023) — Model Solution


Part (c): Elasticities of Demand and Engel Aggregation Condition

Reference: Deaton, A. & Muellbauer, J., Economics and Consumer Behaviour (1980), Ch. 2; Koutsoyiannis, A., Modern Microeconomics, 2nd ed., Ch. 2; Henderson & Quandt, Microeconomic Theory, 3rd ed., Ch. 3.

1. Price Elasticity of Demand

The own-price elasticity of demand for commodity $i$ with respect to its own price $p_i$ measures the proportionate change in demand for $i$ per unit proportionate change in $p_i$, ceteris paribus:

$$\varepsilon_{ii} = \frac{\partial q_i}{\partial p_i} \cdot \frac{p_i}{q_i}$$
  • $|\varepsilon_{ii}| > 1$: elastic demand (luxury/price-sensitive goods)
  • $|\varepsilon_{ii}| = 1$: unit elastic
  • $|\varepsilon_{ii}| < 1$: inelastic demand (necessities)
  • For normal goods: $\varepsilon_{ii} < 0$ (law of demand)

The cross-price elasticity of demand for commodity $i$ with respect to price of $j$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{\partial q_i}{\partial p_j} \cdot \frac{p_j}{q_i}$$

2. Income Elasticity of Demand

The income elasticity of demand for commodity $i$ measures the proportionate change in $q_i$ per unit proportionate change in income $Y$:

$$\eta_i = \frac{\partial q_i}{\partial Y} \cdot \frac{Y}{q_i}$$
  • $\eta_i > 1$: luxury good (demand rises faster than income)
  • $0 < \eta_i < 1$: necessary good
  • $\eta_i < 0$: inferior good (demand falls as income rises)

3. Proof of the Engel Aggregation Condition

Statement: $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \eta_i = 1$, where $w_i = \dfrac{p_i q_i}{Y}$ is the budget share of commodity $i$.

Setup: Consider a consumer with income $Y$ who allocates it among $n$ commodities subject to the budget constraint:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i\, q_i(p_1, \ldots, p_n, Y) = Y \tag{1}$$

where $q_i(\cdot)$ are the Marshallian demand functions. The prices $p_i$ are treated as fixed constants; only income $Y$ varies.

Step 1: Differentiate both sides of the budget constraint (1) with respect to income $Y$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial Y}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i\,q_i\right] = \frac{\partial Y}{\partial Y} = 1$$

Since prices $p_i$ are constants with respect to $Y$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i\,\frac{\partial q_i}{\partial Y} = 1 \tag{2}$$

Step 2: Multiply and divide the $i$-th term by $\dfrac{q_i Y}{q_i Y}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i\,\frac{\partial q_i}{\partial Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i\,q_i}{Y} \cdot \frac{\partial q_i}{\partial Y} \cdot \frac{Y}{q_i} = \sum_{i=1}^n w_i \eta_i$$

Step 3: Substituting back into equation (2):

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\,\eta_i = 1}$$

This is the Engel Aggregation Condition. Q.E.D.

4. Economic Interpretation

  • The Engel condition is an accounting identity derived solely from the budget constraint — it holds for any set of demand functions that exhaust the budget.
  • It implies that the income-weighted average of all income elasticities equals unity. Since income must be fully allocated, a dollar of extra income is exactly distributed among all goods, weighted by their expenditure shares.
  • If all goods were necessities ($\eta_i < 1$), the weighted sum would be less than 1, violating the identity — impossible. Hence, in any budget, at least one good must have $\eta_i > 1$ (a luxury) to maintain the aggregation condition.
  • This condition is used in applied demand analysis to cross-check estimated income elasticities from household expenditure surveys (NSS/NSSO data in India).
[Q.E.D.]
2023.6.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2023) — Model Solution


Part (a): Pareto Income Distribution — Lorenz Curve and Gini Coefficient

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; Atkinson, A. B., The Economics of Inequality; Kendall, Ord & Stuart, The Advanced Theory of Statistics, Vol. 1, Ch. 6.

1. Pareto Distribution of Income

The Pareto law states that the number of individuals $N(x)$ with income greater than or equal to $x$ is:

$$N(x) = A\,x^{-\alpha}, \quad x \geq x_0, \quad \alpha > 1$$

where $A > 0$ is a scale constant and $\alpha > 1$ is the Pareto index (inequality parameter).

The total population: $N_0 = N(x_0) = A\,x_0^{-\alpha}$.

The probability density function (pdf) is obtained by $f(x) = -\frac{1}{N_0}\frac{dN}{dx}$:

$$f(x) = \frac{\alpha\,x_0^{\alpha}}{x^{\alpha+1}}, \quad x \geq x_0$$

This is the standard Pareto Type I distribution with shape parameter $\alpha$ and scale $x_0$.

2. Derivation of the Lorenz Curve

The Lorenz curve $L(p)$ plots, for each cumulative population proportion $p \in [0,1]$, the corresponding cumulative income proportion. We need two quantities: the income threshold $x^*(p)$ corresponding to proportion $p$, and the cumulative income up to $x^*$.

Step 1: CDF and the income threshold $x^*(p)$.

The CDF (proportion of population with income $\leq x$):

$$F(x) = 1 - \left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right)^{\alpha}, \quad x \geq x_0$$

Setting $F(x^*) = p$:

$$1 - \left(\frac{x_0}{x^*}\right)^{\alpha} = p \implies x^* = x_0\,(1-p)^{-1/\alpha}$$

Step 2: Mean income $\mu$.

$$\mu = \int_{x_0}^{\infty} x\,f(x)\,dx = \alpha\,x_0^{\alpha} \int_{x_0}^{\infty} x^{-\alpha}\,dx = \alpha\,x_0^{\alpha} \cdot \frac{x_0^{1-\alpha}}{\alpha-1} = \frac{\alpha\,x_0}{\alpha-1}$$

(This requires $\alpha > 1$ for the mean to exist.)

Step 3: Cumulative income up to threshold $x^*$.

$$\mu\,L(p) = \int_{x_0}^{x^*} x\,f(x)\,dx = \alpha\,x_0^{\alpha} \int_{x_0}^{x^*} x^{-\alpha}\,dx = \alpha\,x_0^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\frac{x^{1-\alpha}}{1-\alpha}\right]_{x_0}^{x^*}$$ $$= \frac{\alpha\,x_0^{\alpha}}{\alpha-1}\left[x_0^{1-\alpha} - (x^*)^{1-\alpha}\right] = \mu\left[1 - \left(\frac{x_0}{x^*}\right)^{\alpha-1}\right]$$

Step 4: Substitute $x^* = x_0(1-p)^{-1/\alpha}$.

$$\left(\frac{x_0}{x^*}\right)^{\alpha-1} = (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}$$

Therefore the Lorenz Curve is:

$$\boxed{L(p) = 1 - (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}}$$

Verification: $L(0) = 1-(1)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha} = 0$ ✓ and $L(1) = 1-0 = 1$ ✓

3. Derivation of the Gini Coefficient

The Gini coefficient is defined as twice the area between the line of perfect equality and the Lorenz curve:

$$G = 1 - 2\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp$$

Step 1: Compute $\displaystyle\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp$:

$$\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp = \int_0^1 \left[1 - (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\right]dp = 1 - \int_0^1 (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\,dp$$

Step 2: Evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^1 (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\,dp$.

Let $u = 1-p$, $du = -dp$; limits change from $p=0 \to u=1$ and $p=1 \to u=0$:

$$\int_0^1 (1-p)^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\,dp = \int_0^1 u^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha}\,du = \left[\frac{u^{(\alpha-1)/\alpha+1}}{(\alpha-1)/\alpha + 1}\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{\frac{\alpha-1+\alpha}{\alpha}} = \frac{\alpha}{2\alpha-1}$$

Step 3: Assemble:

$$\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp = 1 - \frac{\alpha}{2\alpha-1} = \frac{2\alpha-1-\alpha}{2\alpha-1} = \frac{\alpha-1}{2\alpha-1}$$

Step 4:

$$G = 1 - 2\cdot\frac{\alpha-1}{2\alpha-1} = \frac{2\alpha-1 - 2(\alpha-1)}{2\alpha-1} = \frac{2\alpha-1-2\alpha+2}{2\alpha-1} = \frac{1}{2\alpha-1}$$ $$\boxed{G = \frac{1}{2\alpha-1}}$$

4. $\alpha$ as a Measure of Income Inequality

  • As $\alpha \to \infty$: $G \to 0$ — perfect income equality
  • As $\alpha \to 1^+$: $G \to 1$ — extreme income inequality (all income concentrated in the top earner)
  • Larger $\alpha$ $\Rightarrow$ lower Gini $\Rightarrow$ less inequality. Hence $\alpha$ is an inverse measure of inequality.
  • Empirically, $\alpha$ typically lies between 1.5 and 3.5 for income distributions of developed countries, corresponding to Gini values between 0.5 and 0.22 respectively.
  • $\alpha \leq 1$ is not admissible since the mean income becomes infinite.
[Q.E.D.]
2023.6.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2023) — Model Solution


Part (c): Cost of Living Index Number (COLIN) — Construction and Equivalence Proof

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics, Part II; Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers; CSO, Manual on Consumer Price Index; Allen, R. G. D., Index Numbers in Theory and Practice.

1. Concept of the Cost of Living Index (COLI)

The Cost of Living Index Number (COLIN) is a price index that measures the proportionate change in the cost of maintaining a fixed standard of living (fixed basket of goods and services) between the base period and the current period.

It is conceptually equivalent to the answer to: "How much money does a household need in the current period to maintain exactly the same level of well-being as in the base period?"

In India, the primary COLINs are:

  • CPI-IW (Consumer Price Index for Industrial Workers) — Labour Bureau
  • CPI-AL/RL (Agricultural/Rural Labourers) — Labour Bureau
  • CPI (Combined) — CSO/MOSPI, base year 2012

2. Steps in Constructing a COLI

  1. Define the reference population: Identify the population group (e.g., industrial workers in urban areas).
  2. Conduct household expenditure survey: Determine what commodities are consumed and their relative importance (via NSS/Labour Bureau surveys). This gives the commodity basket.
  3. Select commodities and determine weights: Group commodities into major groups (Food, Clothing, Fuel, Housing, Miscellaneous). Weights reflect base-year expenditure proportions.
  4. Select markets/centres: Choose representative markets across the relevant region for price collection.
  5. Collect current-period prices: Gather retail prices from the selected markets at regular intervals (monthly for CPI).
  6. Compute elementary aggregates: Compute group-level price relatives using geometric or arithmetic mean of individual price relatives.
  7. Compute the overall COLI: Aggregate group indices using base-period expenditure shares as weights.

3. Two Methods of COLI Construction

(i) Aggregate Expenditure Method

Directly compare the total expenditure on the base-period basket at current prices versus base-period prices:

$$COLI_{AE} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{0i}\,q_{0i}} \times 100$$

Here $q_{0i}$ is the base-period quantity consumed and $p_{1i}, p_{0i}$ are current and base-period prices. This is directly the Laspeyres formula.

(ii) Family Budget Method

Computes a weighted arithmetic average of individual price relatives, using base-period value expenditure shares as weights:

  • Budget share (weight) of commodity $i$ in the base period: $W_i = \dfrac{p_{0i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_j p_{0j}\,q_{0j}}$
  • Price relative of commodity $i$: $R_i = \dfrac{p_{1i}}{p_{0i}} \times 100$
$$COLI_{FB} = \sum_{i=1}^n W_i\,R_i$$

This method is more transparent — field workers compute price relatives for each commodity, and these are then aggregated using known fixed weights, making it easier to update and publish.

4. Mathematical Proof: Family Budget Method = Laspeyres Index

Claim: $COLI_{FB} = COLI_{AE} = L_{01}$

Proof:

Starting from the Family Budget formula:

$$COLI_{FB} = \sum_{i=1}^n W_i\,R_i = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{p_{0i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_j p_{0j}\,q_{0j}}\right) \times \left(\frac{p_{1i}}{p_{0i}} \times 100\right)$$

Expanding, the $p_{0i}$ cancels in the $i$-th term:

$$= \frac{100}{\sum_j p_{0j}\,q_{0j}} \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{p_{0i}\,q_{0i} \cdot p_{1i}}{p_{0i}} = \frac{100}{\sum_j p_{0j}\,q_{0j}} \sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}\,q_{0i}$$ $$\therefore\quad COLI_{FB} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{1i}\,q_{0i}}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_{0i}\,q_{0i}} \times 100 = COLI_{AE} = L_{01}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Family Budget Method} \equiv \text{Laspeyres Index}}$$

5. Practical Significance

The equivalence shows that the Family Budget Method, despite being operationally different (computing and then weighting price relatives), is theoretically identical to the Aggregate Expenditure Method. The Family Budget approach is preferred in practice because:

  • Weights can be fixed and published once, avoiding repeated collection of quantities
  • Price relatives are easier for field investigators to collect than expenditure aggregates
  • Individual commodity price relatives can be published, allowing users to see which items are driving the index
[Q.E.D.]

2024.2.2:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (b): Fixed Base vs. Chain Base Index Numbers

1. Definitions and Fundamental Distinction

Fixed Base Index Number (FBI): An index number in which a single base period, selected once and kept constant throughout the entire series, is used as the reference point for all comparisons. If period 0 is the fixed base, then the index for period $t$ is:

$$P_t = \frac{p_t}{p_0} \times 100$$

All temporal comparisons are thus made against the same period $p_0$.

Chain Base Index Number (CBI): An index number in which the immediately preceding period serves as the base for each successive period. The index for period $t$, called the link relative, is:

$$L_t = \frac{p_t}{p_{t-1}} \times 100$$

Long-run comparisons are made by chaining (multiplying sequentially) these link relatives.

2. Relative Merits and Demerits

FeatureFixed BaseChain Base
Stability of comparison All comparisons anchored to one period — consistent. Each period compared to its predecessor — less stable over the long run.
Updating flexibility Adding new commodities requires revision from the base year onwards — cumbersome. New commodities and weights can be incorporated at any link — highly flexible.
Base obsolescence If the base year is abnormal (war, pandemic), comparisons become misleading over time. Never suffers from an outdated base; each link reflects current conditions.
Long-period accuracy Reliable for long-period trend measurement (no chaining error). Chaining errors may accumulate over many links.
Computational effort Straightforward once weights and base prices are fixed. Requires sequential multiplication — slightly more involved but manageable.
Practical use CSO Consumer Price Index (CPI), WPI use fixed base updated periodically. EU Harmonised Index, OECD chain-linking methods.

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics, Part II; Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers.

3. Conversion Formula: Chain Base → Fixed Base

Let the chain base index for year $t$ be $L_t$ (link relative) and let the fixed base index for year $t$ be $F_t$ (base year = 100). The conversion relationship is:

$$F_t = \frac{F_{t-1} \times L_t}{100}$$

We start by setting $F_{\text{base}} = 100$ for the chosen fixed base year.

4. Numerical Calculation

Given data (Chain Base Index Numbers):

Year2005200620072008200920102011
CBI ($L_t$)9810211513512510395

Fixed Base: 2005 = 100. (The $L_{2005} = 98$ implies 2005 is at 98 relative to 2004. We adopt 2005 as our reference, so $F_{2005} = 100$ by convention.)

  • $F_{2005} = 100.00$ (fixed base)
  • $F_{2006} = \dfrac{100 \times 102}{100} = 102.00$
  • $F_{2007} = \dfrac{102 \times 115}{100} = 117.30$
  • $F_{2008} = \dfrac{117.30 \times 135}{100} = \dfrac{15835.5}{100} = 158.36$ (rounded to 2 d.p.)
  • $F_{2009} = \dfrac{158.36 \times 125}{100} = \dfrac{19794.9}{100} = 197.95$
  • $F_{2010} = \dfrac{197.95 \times 103}{100} = \dfrac{20388.9}{100} = 203.89$
  • $F_{2011} = \dfrac{203.89 \times 95}{100} = \dfrac{19369.5}{100} = 193.70$

Summary Table (Fixed Base, 2005 = 100):

YearCBI ($L_t$)Fixed Base Index ($F_t$)
200598100.00
2006102102.00
2007115117.30
2008135158.36
2009125197.95
2010103203.89
201195193.70

Interpretation: By 2011, the price level (fixed base 2005 = 100) stood at 193.70, meaning prices approximately doubled relative to 2005. The decline from 203.89 in 2010 to 193.70 in 2011 reflects a deflation of about 5% in the terminal year.

[Q.E.D.]
2024.2.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (c): Components of National Income — Expenditure Approach

1. Concept

The Expenditure Approach (also called the Product Expenditure Method) measures National Income by summing the total final expenditure on goods and services produced in the economy during a reference year. It rests on the accounting identity that every unit of output produced must be purchased by some economic agent.

The fundamental identity is:

$$\text{GDP at Market Prices} = C + I + G + (X - M)$$

where each component is defined and calculated as follows.

2. Components and Their Calculation

(i) Private Final Consumption Expenditure ($C$)

Expenditure by resident households and non-profit institutions serving households (NPISH) on final goods and services, both domestic and imported. It is the largest component in most economies ($\approx$ 55–60% of GDP in India).

  • Includes: food, clothing, education, health, recreation, housing services (imputed rent)
  • Excludes: purchase of land, buildings, or financial assets (these are saving/investment)
  • Estimation (India — CSO/MOSPI): Commodity flow method + Retail trade surveys + NSS household consumption expenditure surveys
(ii) Gross Fixed Capital Formation ($I$) — Investment

Total expenditure on fixed assets (machinery, equipment, structures, intellectual property) by producers plus changes in inventories (work-in-progress, finished goods, raw materials).

$$I = \text{Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF)} + \Delta \text{Inventories}$$
  • GFCF covers both private and government investment
  • Estimation: Investment surveys, Capital goods production data, Infrastructure project data from Budget documents
(iii) Government Final Consumption Expenditure ($G$)

Current expenditure by general government on goods and services (excluding transfer payments such as subsidies, pensions — these are not production-related expenditures).

  • Includes: salaries of government employees, expenditure on defence, administration, public health, education
  • Transfer payments (subsidies, interest payments) are excluded as they do not correspond to production
  • Estimation: Government Budget accounts and Finance Accounts of Union and States
(iv) Net Exports ($X - M$)

The difference between:

  • Exports ($X$): Value of goods and services produced domestically and sold to the rest of the world
  • Imports ($M$): Value of goods and services purchased from the rest of the world

Net Exports can be positive (trade surplus) or negative (trade deficit).

$$\text{Net Exports} = X - M$$
  • Estimation: DGCI&S merchandise trade data + RBI Balance of Payments data for invisible trade

3. Complete Identity and Transition from GDP to NNP/NI

Starting from GDP at market prices:

$$\text{GDP}_{MP} = C + I + G + (X - M)$$

Adjustments to reach Net National Product (NNP) at factor cost (National Income):

$$\text{NNP}_{FC} = \text{GDP}_{MP} - \text{Depreciation} + \text{Net Factor Income from Abroad} - \text{Net Indirect Taxes}$$

where $\text{Net Indirect Taxes} = \text{Indirect Taxes} - \text{Subsidies}$.

In matrix form:

$$\text{National Income (NI)} = C + I + G + (X - M) - D + NFIA - NIT$$

4. Double Counting and the Role of 'Final' Expenditure

Only final expenditure is counted. Intermediate goods (e.g., steel purchased by a car manufacturer) are excluded to avoid double counting. This is operationalised by summing expenditure only at the last stage of production or by using the value-added method as a cross-check.

5. India's Implementation (MOSPI/CSO)

  • India follows the United Nations System of National Accounts (UN SNA 2008) framework
  • Base year is currently 2011–12
  • GDP estimates are released in Advance Estimate, First Revised Estimate, and Second Revised Estimate series
  • The expenditure approach GDP aligns with the income and production approach GDP through statistical discrepancies
[Q.E.D.]
2024.5.1:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (a): Identification Problem in Simultaneous Equations

1. The Identification Problem — Concept

In a system of simultaneous structural equations, the identification problem asks: can the structural parameters of a given equation be uniquely recovered from the reduced form parameters?

The problem arises because the reduced form (obtained by solving the structural form) yields a finite set of estimable parameters, but multiple distinct structural forms may produce the same reduced form. Thus, without additional restrictions, we cannot uniquely attribute an estimated relationship to one specific structural equation.

Reference: Gujarati & Porter, Basic Econometrics, 5th ed., Chapter 18; Johnston & DiNardo, Econometric Methods, 4th ed., Chapter 9.

2. Notation and System Setup

Consider a system of $G$ structural equations with:

  • $G$ = total number of equations (= number of endogenous variables)
  • $M$ = number of endogenous variables included in a particular equation
  • $K$ = total number of predetermined (exogenous + lagged endogenous) variables in the entire system
  • $K^*$ = number of predetermined variables excluded from the equation under consideration
  • $m$ = number of endogenous variables excluded from the equation

3. Conditions for Identification

(i) Order Condition (Necessary but not Sufficient)

An equation is identified if the number of variables (endogenous and exogenous) excluded from the equation is at least as large as the number of equations in the system minus one:

$$K^* \geq G - 1$$
  • If $K^* = G - 1$: equation is exactly identified (just-identified)
  • If $K^* > G - 1$: equation is over-identified
  • If $K^* < G - 1$: equation is under-identified (not identified)
(ii) Rank Condition (Necessary and Sufficient)

The equation is identified if and only if it is possible to construct at least one non-zero determinant of order $(G-1) \times (G-1)$ from the coefficients of the variables excluded from the equation but included in the other equations.

Formally: The rank of the matrix $A$ formed by the coefficients of excluded variables (from the equation in question) in the remaining equations must equal $G - 1$.

4. Application: Examination of the Supply-Demand Model

The structural model:

$$Q_d = \beta_0 + \beta_1 P + U \quad \text{(Demand equation)} \tag{1}$$ $$Q_s = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 P + V \quad \text{(Supply equation)} \tag{2}$$ $$Q_d = Q_s = Q \quad \text{(Equilibrium condition)} \tag{3}$$

Assumptions used:

  • $Q$ (quantity) and $P$ (price) are both endogenous variables — they are jointly determined within the system.
  • There are no exogenous or predetermined variables in either equation.
  • $U, V$ are random disturbances with standard properties: $E(U) = E(V) = 0$, uncorrelated with each other.

System parameters:

  • $G = 2$ structural equations
  • Endogenous variables: $Q, P$ — total 2
  • Predetermined variables: None ($K = 0$)

Identification analysis of the Demand Equation (1):

  • Variables in the demand equation: $Q, P$ (both endogenous)
  • Variables excluded from the demand equation: None ($K^* = 0$)
  • Order condition: $K^* \geq G - 1 \Rightarrow 0 \geq 1$ — FAILS
  • Result: Demand equation is NOT IDENTIFIED (under-identified)

Identification analysis of the Supply Equation (2):

  • Variables in the supply equation: $Q, P$ (both endogenous)
  • Variables excluded from the supply equation: None ($K^* = 0$)
  • Order condition: $K^* \geq G - 1 \Rightarrow 0 \geq 1$ — FAILS
  • Result: Supply equation is NOT IDENTIFIED (under-identified)

5. Economic Intuition

Since both $Q$ and $P$ are endogenous and there are no exogenous variables to "shift" only the demand curve or only the supply curve, the observed equilibrium data points $(P_t, Q_t)$ lie on both curves simultaneously. The scatter plot of $(P, Q)$ therefore traces neither the demand curve nor the supply curve but some mixture of both — there is no way to separate them econometrically.

Remedy: Introduce at least one exogenous variable (e.g., consumer income $Y$ in the demand equation, or input cost $W$ in the supply equation). Then the excluded variable creates an exclusion restriction, and the order condition becomes satisfiable.

For example, if consumer income $Y$ is added only to the demand equation:

$$Q_d = \beta_0 + \beta_1 P + \beta_2 Y + U \quad (K^* = 1 = G-1 = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Supply is exactly identified})$$ [Q.E.D.]
2024.7.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (b): Income Elasticity of Demand — Estimation from Family-Budget Data

1. Definition

The income elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in consumer income, holding all prices constant. Formally:

$$\eta_Y = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial Y} \cdot \frac{Y}{Q}$$

where $Q$ = quantity demanded and $Y$ = consumer income.

Reference: Koutsoyiannis, A., Modern Microeconomics, 2nd ed., Chapter 2; Henderson & Quandt, Microeconomic Theory, 3rd ed.

Classification of goods by income elasticity:

  • $\eta_Y > 1$: Luxury goods (income-elastic) — demand rises more than proportionately with income
  • $0 < \eta_Y < 1$: Normal necessities — demand rises less than proportionately with income
  • $\eta_Y = 0$: Income-inelastic goods — demand unresponsive to income (e.g., salt)
  • $\eta_Y < 0$: Inferior goods — demand falls as income rises (e.g., coarse cereals)

2. Estimation from Family-Budget Data

Family-budget surveys (such as the NSS Household Consumer Expenditure Survey in India) record income $Y_i$ and expenditure on a commodity $E_i$ (or quantity $Q_i$) for each household $i$, along with household size $n_i$.

Method 1: Double-Log (Log-Linear) Regression — Engel Curve Approach

Assume the demand function is of constant-elasticity form:

$$Q = A \cdot Y^{\eta_Y} \cdot e^u$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln Q_i = \ln A + \eta_Y \ln Y_i + u_i$$

This is a simple linear regression of $\ln Q_i$ on $\ln Y_i$. The OLS estimator of the slope $\hat{\eta}_Y$ is the estimated income elasticity. This method gives a constant elasticity over the entire income range.

Method 2: Semi-Log (Semilogarithmic) Engel Curve

Sometimes the Working-Leser specification is preferred:

$$w_i = \alpha + \beta \ln Y_i + v_i$$

where $w_i = E_i / Y_i$ is the budget share. The income elasticity is then:

$$\eta_Y = \frac{\beta}{w} + 1$$

where $w$ is the mean budget share. This is the basis of the Working-Leser model widely used in CSO/NSSO demand analysis.

Method 3: Linear Engel Curve
$$E_i = a + b Y_i + u_i$$

Then:

$$\eta_Y = b \cdot \frac{\bar{Y}}{\bar{E}}$$

where $\bar{Y}$ and $\bar{E}$ are sample means of income and expenditure respectively, and $b$ is the OLS estimate of the marginal propensity to spend on that commodity.

3. Adjustments for Variation in Family Size

A critical issue in cross-section family-budget data is that large families tend to have higher total income and higher total expenditure even if their per-capita welfare is lower. This creates family-size bias in the estimation of income elasticity.

Standard adjustments are as follows:

(i) Per Capita (Adult Equivalent) Scaling

Replace household income $Y_i$ and expenditure $E_i$ by their per-adult-equivalent values:

$$y_i = \frac{Y_i}{m_i}, \quad q_i = \frac{Q_i}{m_i}$$

where $m_i$ is the number of adult equivalents in household $i$ (calculated using an equivalence scale such as the OECD scale or CSO norms). Then regress $\ln q_i$ on $\ln y_i$.

(ii) Including Family Size as a Regressor

Directly include $\ln n_i$ (log of household size) in the regression:

$$\ln Q_i = \alpha + \eta_Y \ln Y_i + \gamma \ln n_i + u_i$$

The coefficient $\eta_Y$ then measures the pure income effect holding family size constant, and $\gamma$ captures the household-size effect.

(iii) Stratification by Family Size

Divide the sample into strata by family size (e.g., 1–2 persons, 3–4 persons, 5+ persons) and estimate separate Engel curves within each stratum. Then pool the estimates using weighted regression. This is computationally intensive but avoids parametric assumptions about the form of the family-size effect.

(iv) Prais-Houthakker Specific-Scaling Method

Prais & Houthakker (1955) proposed commodity-specific scaling factors $\phi_c$ for each commodity $c$. The effective household size for commodity $c$ is $m_i(c) = \phi_c \cdot n_i^{\theta_c}$, estimated iteratively. This is the most theoretically rigorous approach.

4. Practical Note (NSSO Application)

The NSS Household Consumer Expenditure Survey (Schedule 1.0) is the principal data source in India. Income elasticities are typically estimated commodity-wise using the double-log Engel curve after converting to per-capita monthly expenditure (MPCE), stratified by rural/urban sector.

[Q.E.D.]
2024.8.4:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (i): Types of Errors in Index Numbers

Index numbers, being statistical constructs that summarise complex price and quantity movements in a single number, are susceptible to several types of errors arising from conceptual, statistical, and practical limitations. These errors reduce the accuracy and representativeness of the index.

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; Fisher, I., The Making of Index Numbers; CSO Manual on Index Numbers.

1. Formula Bias (Method Bias)

Arises from the choice of index number formula. Different formulas give different results even with identical data:

  • Upward bias of Laspeyres: Laspeyres index ($L$) uses base-year quantities as weights. Since consumers substitute away from goods that become relatively more expensive, the base-year basket overstates the cost of living. Therefore $L \geq P$ (Paasche), known as the Laspeyres bias.
  • Downward bias of Paasche: Paasche index uses current-year quantities, which already reflect substitution. It understates the true cost of maintaining base-year welfare. Mathematically: $P \leq L$ (Bortkiewicz inequality).
  • Fisher's Ideal: $F = \sqrt{L \times P}$ is designed to cancel formula bias as it satisfies both the time-reversal and factor-reversal tests.

2. Sampling Errors in Price and Quantity Collection

Prices and quantities are usually collected by sampling rather than complete enumeration of all transactions. This introduces:

  • Sampling error: Variability due to random selection of commodities, markets, or respondents; quantifiable and controllable
  • Non-response error: Certain vendors or markets may not report data, leading to systematic gaps
  • Coverage error: If the market basket does not cover all relevant goods (e.g., e-commerce transactions excluded from WPI), the index is incomplete

3. Base Period Errors

The choice of base period critically affects the index:

  • Abnormal base year: If the base year coincides with an exceptional event (war, pandemic, drought), prices in that year are atypical. Subsequent index values will be distorted relative to "normal" conditions.
  • Outdated base: As the economy changes structurally (new products, changed consumption patterns), an old base year becomes increasingly unrepresentative. This is remedied by periodic base-year revision (India's WPI was revised from 2004–05 to 2011–12 base).

4. Weighting Errors

Weights in an index represent the relative importance of each commodity. Weighting errors arise when:

  • Incorrect weights: Weights are based on outdated consumption surveys or production data that no longer reflect current expenditure patterns
  • Quality change ignored: If a product improves in quality but its price stays the same, the true purchasing power gain is not captured — a major issue in CPI measurement (hedonic adjustment is the remedy)
  • New goods bias: New products (e.g., smartphones in the 1990s) are excluded from the index until a revision, causing the index to overstate inflation
  • Outlet substitution bias: Consumers shift purchases from high-price traditional outlets to discount/online stores; if the index samples only traditional outlets, it overstates prices

5. Measurement / Reporting Errors

  • Price quotation errors: Prices collected may be list prices rather than actual transaction prices (discounts, deals ignored)
  • Seasonal adjustment errors: Improper seasonal correction distorts the index series
  • Aggregation errors: Using average prices when geometric mean of prices would be more appropriate (Jevons approach vs. Dutot approach for elementary aggregates)

6. Chaining Errors

In chain-linked indices, successive multiplication of link relatives accumulates rounding errors and may produce chain drift — a spurious trend in the index unrelated to actual price movements — especially when prices are highly volatile.

Summary

Error TypeSourceRemedy
Formula BiasIndex formula choiceUse Fisher's Ideal / superlative index
Sampling ErrorIncomplete price collectionProbability-based price sampling
Base Period ErrorAbnormal/outdated base yearRegular base-year revision
Weighting ErrorOutdated or wrong weightsUpdate weights from recent surveys
New Goods BiasOmission of new productsIntroduce new items at each revision
Quality Change BiasUnaccounted quality improvementHedonic pricing methods
Chaining ErrorAccumulation in chain indicesAnnual chain-linking with benchmarks
[Q.E.D.]
2024.8.5:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2024) — Model Solution


Part (ii): Real Wage Comparison using Cost of Living Index

Given Data

  • Cost of Living Index (COLI): rises from 110 to 200
  • Worker's salary: rises from ₹500 to ₹900

1. Theoretical Framework

The real wage (or real salary) measures the purchasing power of a nominal salary in terms of a reference price level. When the cost of living changes, a nominal salary increase may not represent a genuine improvement in living standards.

The real salary at any point, measured in terms of the base period price level (when COLI = 100), is:

$$\text{Real Salary} = \frac{\text{Nominal Salary}}{\text{COLI}} \times 100$$

Reference: Bowley, A. L., Elements of Statistics; CSO Manual on Price Indices.

2. Calculation of Real Wages

Real salary before the change (when COLI = 110, Salary = ₹500):

$$\text{Real Salary (Before)} = \frac{500}{110} \times 100 = \frac{50000}{110} = ₹454.55$$

Real salary after the change (when COLI = 200, Salary = ₹900):

$$\text{Real Salary (After)} = \frac{900}{200} \times 100 = \frac{90000}{200} = ₹450.00$$

3. Assessment: Does the Worker Really Gain?

Comparing real salaries:

$$\text{Change in Real Salary} = 450.00 - 454.55 = -₹4.55$$

The worker does NOT gain in real terms. On the contrary, the worker suffers a real loss of ₹4.55 (measured at the base period price level).

4. Alternative Verification: Required Salary to Maintain Purchasing Power

To maintain the same real purchasing power as before (when salary = ₹500 and COLI = 110), the salary should have been increased to:

$$\text{Required Salary} = 500 \times \frac{200}{110} = 500 \times 1.8182 = ₹909.09$$

The actual salary paid is ₹900, which is less than the required ₹909.09 by:

$$\text{Shortfall} = 909.09 - 900.00 = ₹9.09$$

This means the salary raise of ₹400 (from ₹500 to ₹900) is insufficient to compensate for the rise in cost of living from 110 to 200. The worker needs an additional ₹9.09 to restore the original standard of living.

5. Percentage Change in Real Wage

$$\% \text{ change in real wage} = \frac{450.00 - 454.55}{454.55} \times 100 = \frac{-4.55}{454.55} \times 100 \approx -1.0\%$$

The real wage has fallen by approximately 1%, indicating a mild erosion of purchasing power.

6. Conclusion

Despite an apparent nominal gain of ₹400 (80% increase in salary), the cost of living has risen by approximately 81.8% (from index 110 to 200). Since the salary increase (80%) is less than the increase in the cost of living (81.8%), the worker is worse off in real terms by ₹9.09 per month (at nominal prices) or ₹4.55 at base-period prices.

[Q.E.D.]

2025.1.3:
UPSC ISS Statistics Paper III (2025) — Model Solution


1. Definition of Constant Price Elasticity of Demand

The price elasticity of demand, denoted by $\eta$, is defined as the ratio of the relative change in quantity demanded ($q$) to the relative change in price ($p$):

$$ \eta = -\frac{dq/q}{dp/p} = -\frac{p}{q} \cdot \frac{dq}{dp} $$

For a demand curve with constant price elasticity, we set $\eta = k$, where $k$ is a positive constant ($k > 0$). Thus:

$$ -\frac{p}{q} \cdot \frac{dq}{dp} = k $$

2. Derivation of the Demand Function

To find the functional form of the demand curve, we rearrange the differential equation and integrate with respect to $p$:

$$ \frac{dq}{q} = -k \frac{dp}{p} $$

Integrating both sides:

$$ \int \frac{1}{q} dq = -k \int \frac{1}{p} dp $$ $$ \ln(q) = -k \ln(p) + C $$

where $C$ is the constant of integration. Exponentiating both sides:

$$ q = e^{-k \ln(p) + C} = e^C \cdot e^{\ln(p^{-k})} $$

Let $A = e^C$ (where $A > 0$ is a scale parameter). The demand function becomes:

$$ q = A p^{-k} $$

3. Analysis of the Shape of the Curve

The equation $q = A p^{-k}$ represents the demand curve. We analyze its shape based on the parameter $k$:

  • General Case: The equation $q = A p^{-k}$ is a power function. In the $(p, q)$ plane, this is a generalized hyperbola. As $p \to 0$, $q \to \infty$, and as $p \to \infty$, $q \to 0$. The curvature and the rate of decline are governed by the magnitude of $k$.
  • Case $k=1$: When the elasticity of demand is unity ($\eta = 1$), the equation simplifies to: $$ q = A p^{-1} \implies q = \frac{A}{p} $$ or $$ pq = A $$

4. Proof for the Equilateral Hyperbola

An equilateral hyperbola in the Cartesian coordinate system is defined by the equation $xy = c^2$, where the asymptotes are the axes of the coordinate system. In our derived demand function for $k=1$:

$$ pq = A $$

This is mathematically identical to the standard form of an equilateral hyperbola $xy = A$, where $p$ corresponds to the $x$-axis and $q$ corresponds to the $y$-axis. The product of price and quantity is constant ($A$), which represents total expenditure.
